column1: 5215,object05215,record,,,,,Sackville Town Book,,"Sackville New Brunswick, 1748-1883, (MG 9, A 12-6) Public Archives of Canada",,,,,1748
At a  meeting of the Committee for the Township of Sackville in Nova Scotia the 20th Day of July, A.D. 1762 at the house of Mrs Charity Bishop Innholder at Cumberland
The Committee made choice of Cap. John Huston, Esq. for their Charman and Mr. Ichabod Comstock, Clerk..
Resolved that six in Family and seven Hed of cattle or other Stock Aquivalent thereto Shall have one Share and a half.
RESOLVED that John  Park be admitted to one Share and a half
William Jenkes, one Share and a half
RESOLVED that there be Two Hundred House Lotts laid out on West Cock Hill Consisting of half an Acre to Each Lott.
RESOLVED that West Cock Hill and the Land Adjacent Excluding the Town Lotts, as also West Cock Marsh be Divided so as to Accomodate these fifty shares following Viz
column2: Doctr John Jenkes, Edmund Jenkes, John Olney, John Park, William Maxwell, William Alverson, Joshua Sprague, Stephen Jenkes, Ichabod Comstock, Joseph Winsor, David Smith, William Olney’ Samuel Currie, Benjamin Thurber, Jonathan Jenkes,

column1: PAGE TWO: Peter Williams. Charles Olney, James Olney, John Foster, William Jenkes, Nicholas Cook and Col William Brown, Share and a Half and James Jenkes, David Alverson, Samuel Saunders, Nathanial Finney, Ja? Finney, Coggehall Olney, Timothey Williams, David Fast, Benjamin Willboar, Samuel Rogers, Isaac Cole, Charles Arnold, Samuel Ballou, Amariah Leland, Robert Foster, and, Caleb Shearman, One Share and Ezekial Fuller and, John Cole half a Share....And to take the remainder of their proportional part of the diked marsh on the East Side of the River Tintemar below the Old Bridge Place.

AT A MEETING of the Committee for the Township of Sackville in Nova Scotia the 31st Day of August, A.D. 1792 at the House of Mrs. Charity Bishop Innholder at Cumberland, present John Huston Esq. Chairman
WHEREAS at the last meeting it was resolv’d that there Shou’d be Fifty Shares in West Cock Division, Now Resolved that there be Five Shares more Enaxed

RESOLVED that the Lower Mill Creek be the Dividing Line between West Cock Village and the Midle Village Untill it Comes to the Hed of the Marsh as it appears By a Plan drawn by Col William Brown. From thence Northwest untill it meets the eastern Line of the Township, And that the other boundry of the Midle Village begin at the Old Mill Dam at the Upper Mill Creek as far as there is any Intervail.

From thence Northwest untill it meets Said Extream Line, and that the Land lying South or Southwesterly of the Midle Village or so much thereof as to make Fifty Five Equal to Each Share in the Midle Village to be the Bounderies of West Cock Village....and that the line lying to the Northward of the Upper Mill Creek of so much more thereof as to make sixty six Shares equal to each Share in the Midle Village be the Boundary of the Upper Village.....

RESOLVED: that the land that lyse below the Southward most Line of West Cock Village be divided into Two Hundred Equal Shares, And also that Land that Land that lyeth above the Northwardly Line of the Upper Village be Divided into Two Hundred Equal Shares….
RESOLVED that the aforesaid Lines have no reference to the Marsh or Dik’d landsContained in Said Township
column2: but are the Dividing lines of the Wilderness Part thereof.

column1: Mr Jeremiah Ward having One Share Now admitted to half a Share more . Mr Nicholas Cook having one Share now admitted to half a Share more.

At a town meeting held in the Temperance Hall on Saturday May 1, 1858. James Anderson Esqu. was called to the Chair. Resolved: That a Committee be appointed to wait on Samuel L. Black Esq concerning a school lot and that William Moris, Thomas Baird, Charles Knapp do compose the said committee. . . .

RESOLVED That James Ayer do receive ten shillings for month of September to the present time. Resolved: That M. Ward  do receive...five shillings out of the surplus money collected from the Parish Rate. Resolved: That the sum of the Parish ten shillings be paid Charles Palmer for his services for  laying out a road at the South Joggins.  Resolved: That the sum of five pounds be received from the School Lands Fund if the same amount is raised by subscription for repairing the Hall and that Harris Sears, Alden Trueman, Benje. Campbell, William Morris do compose a Committee for this above purpose.  Amos Ogden Town Clerk
At a town meeting held in the Temperance Hall in Sackville April 30, 1859 William Fawcett was Chairman.

column2: Certain Judgements have been obtained in the Supreme Court of New Brunswick against John Smith and Silas Smith and in the name and inform of Joseph L. Allison for certain debts due the School Land Committee of the Parish on act of the Rent of School Lands

column1: THEREFOR RESOLVED: That Joseph L. Allison transfer the said judgements to C.E. Knapp Esq who obtained the same and the said C.E. Knapp be authorized to deal with the said Judgements as may seem best and pay over the Residue of the same when collected after deducting costs of School land Committee and that said C.E. Knapp fine Joseph L. Allison a discharge from all the costs of the said Judgements in former Resolutions. Passed.

PAGE 115:  Resolved that a committee be appointed to take charge of the Upper Burial Yard and that William Kinnear , Cle. Harper, Titus Hicks do compose the said committee.   Resolved:  That Lewis Kinnear be apointed as Sexton for like Upper Burial Yard.

IN ACCORDANCE with a resolution passed at a Town Meeting held in James Ayer Shop in Sackville on the 30th  of April 1864 it was resolve, and passed the meeting, that Thomas Anderson , Titus Hicks and Amos Ogden School Land Committee should be authorised to ascertain the original boundaries of the School lot of Marsh on the Sunken Island body of Marsh now in dispute with the executors of the late Silas Trueman and to establish and settle the boundaries between the lots we the School Land Committee did in accordance with the resolution employ Charles G, Palmer Surveyor and by and between Stephen Trueman Executor of the late Silas Trueman with the said committee did establish the boundaries of the said lot as follows . Commencing at the east angle of lands belonging to Silas Truman Estate and Martin Trueman there southwesterly parallel with the line dividing the School Lot and Robert and James Trueman Lands straight crossed  to the old A... river.  Signed Thomas Anderson, Titus Hicks, Amos Ogden School Land Committee.

AT A TOWN meeting held in the Agriculture Hall on Tuesday the 6th of December C. Miner Esq. was called to the Chair. Resolved: the sum of twenty dollars be appropriated  for to purchase a stove for the New School House in Westcock now in the course of erection. Amos Ogden, Town Clerk","Transcribed from microfilm for the Tantramar Heritage Trust by Amy Fox, July 2010.","Sackville

column1: 5241,object05241,record,,,,,"Carlyle's First Love Margaret Gordon Lady Bannerman: An Account of her Life, Ancestry, and Homes.","Archibald, Raymond Clare",,,London,John Lane the Bodley Head,,1910,pp. 119-120,Book

Sir Alexander Bannerman arrived in Halifax on February 26, 1851. No railroads had yet been built in the Mariitme Provinces and he left the city the very next day on the hundred and seventy mile coach or sleigh drive to Cape Tormentine. Winter communications with Prince Edward island over the Straits of Northumberland at this time could only be carried on by means of “ice boats” between Cape Tormentine  on the mainland and Cape Traverse on the Island. After stopping overnight at Truro and Amherst, Sir Alexander finally arrived at Cape Tormentine on March 3. He was detained here at the inn of one Tom Allan (footnote), until Saturday March 8, when he crossed the Strait in an ice  boat in about five hours. On landing , Sir Alexander was met by the Colonial Secretary and a large number of inhabitants.

(footnote) The  Tom Allan here referred to , an interesting and active old man of eighty-six, died last summer.  He is a well known character throughout the Maritime Provinces, having had charge of the winter mail service between the Capes for a period of sixty years. He well remembered Sir Alexander, whom he met on his arrival in  Amherst. Having spent the night at Coffey’s Hotel, the Governor told Allan to request the High Sheriff, Minister and other dignitaries to call on him, before they would have to set out for Cape Tormentine. But Tom evidently thought His Excellency should have a much better “send-off,” so as he went through the town, he told every one he met that his presence was desired by the Governor, until over a hundred people appeared at the hotel in response to the summons. Sitr Alexander took the prank in the best of good humour, and heartily welcomed all “just as if he were one of ourselves.”
During the halt at Cape Tormentine, it is related that Sir Alexander one day asled Allan if there were not some game to be found in the Woods nearby. “Oh yes, Governor” replied Tom
column2: “we will get some partridge tomorrow.” Before setting out, Tom abstracted some hens from a nearby henhouse and set them loose in the woods. The sportsmen entered the woods cautiously from another point, and Tom soon pointed out the tracks in the snow of ""a partridge"" surely near at hand. Sir Alexander’s shot was successful. He expressed surprise that “the partridge” was black, but Tom readily explained that “all partridges were black in winter and grey in summer.” Shortly after their return, Sir Alexander was descended upon by the irate lady whose hen had been shot. Tom was caught
column3: but the joke was taken in good part, and he received, from one of the numerous cases brought from Scotland, a “bottle” wherewith to drink the Governor’s good health.

column1: **************************************************************** From time to time, as Allan came to Charlottetown wiht the mails he would ""have a chat"" with the Governor
column2: and when Lady Bannerman arrived, he received a special invitation to Government House to meet her: ""I never heard anyone laugh like her in my life. She kept it up the whole time I was there"" - and anyone who has met Tom Allen could well believe his statement.",,"Allan, Tom

column1: 5285,object05285,record,,,,,Sackville News: Site of Former Activity Last Century - Preceptress at Ladies College - Special Services Being Held by the Methodists,"Smith, Alfred Dean",Saint John Globe,,,,,"February 22, 1909",p.3,Microform,"Ft: Excerpt: . . . .The section of Main  Street extending from Foundry Lane up to the Standard Manufacturing Company Establishment was about the middle of the last century the scene of commercial and industrial activities which the place no longer knows.  Quite a number of stores and workshops were then strung along this thoroughfare, mainly on the western side. On the Palmer property, Jabez Palmer, brother of the late Judge of that name, conducted a retail store. A stone's throw from this stood the large store, now moved back and turned into a barn, in which Mariner Wood, the Senator's father accumulated a large fortune, and not far from it, still further along, Robert Hallett, who came hither from Sussex and married a Miss Boultenhouse, did business for several years.  Then came Butcher's Cabinet Maker's shop, and a general store kept by Nathan Lawrence.  Across the road, Edward Buck carried on a harness shop. Next to Lawrence's Store on the western side again were two blacksmith's shops, Robert Bell's and Ainsley Bowser's.  Then came Silas Black's Carriage factory, at which more or less work is still done by his sons.  Not far to the southward stood Laban Lawrence's flour store, and just this side of the Presbyterian Church was Travis's Carriage factory. Then beside Thomas Babcock's house now occupied by Dr. Andrews, was his retail shop, a building which was subsequently moved down to the corner of Foundry Lane and Main Street where it is used as a store by the Fowler Brothers.  On the Land next to the Babcock property, now occupied by Willie Fawcett, stood A. Estabrook's carpentry shop and on the opposite side of the road, Richard Wilson's store. On the northern corner of the property at present owned and occupied by Dr. Knapp, was David Purington's store,  now moved acrosss the hollow to the northward and converted into a tenement house.  A little to the northward of Walter Fowler's House, Marcus Trueman had a carpenter's shop and, last of all across the road, a little to the southward, was Charles Fawcett's Tin Shop, the place where he started his successful business career. This building was a few years ago moved acrosss the road 500 or 600 feet to the south and is still standing.",,"Main Street

5291,object05291,record,,,,,Fire Adds Tragic Chapter to Story of One of the Oldest Businesses in the Province,,The White Fence,,,,No. 19.,"2002, May|1939",p.5,Periodical
Fire Adds Tragic Chapter to Story of one of Oldest Businesses In the Province
Firm of Joseph L. Black and Sons Organized 111 Years Ago by Samuel Freeze Black

The early Sunday morning fire at Middle Sackville which destroyed the main business establishment of Joseph L. Black and Sons, Limited, causing an estimated loss of $75,000, added a tragic chapter to the story of one of New Brunswick's oldest business establishments. The firm of Joseph L. Black and Sons was organized 111 years ago by the late Samuel Freeze Black who was born in Amherst in 1806 and moved to Middle Sackville in 1828, where he opened a store on the east side of the main highway. He also had a store at Shemogue.

He engaged in shipbuilding and for a time operated his own ships carrying the countries products to outside markets. His nephew, Joseph L. Black, came from Amherst to Sackville and entered Mount Allison Academy which had just been founded by the late Charles F. Allison. After studying at the Academy for a year he apprenticed himself to his Uncle Samuel and later bought him out and continued the business which now bears his name and has been carried on through four generations.

Although the firm's letterhead bears the date of 1847 in its legend, the business was actually established in 1828, 111 years ago. At the time Samuel Black started his store in Middle Sackville it was the main business section of the district. Mail and stage coaches running from Saint John to Halifax went through Middle Sackville to cross the Tantramar and pass on through Jolicure, Point de Bute and Amherst. The present Trans-Atlantic highway which runs from Sackville across the marshes over the Fort Beausejour and Fort Lawrence Ridges was not in existence.

When shipbuilding declined the firm went into the manufacture of lumber, trading in general merchandise and farming in a very considerable scale. The volume of business has grown steadily and soundly and covers the major part of Westmorland County. Lumber products have been shipped chiefly to the British market with some to the United States and Canadian cities. The average annual cut of lumber is from 2,000,000 to 3,000,000 feet and is taken from freehold property.

The first store occupied by Samuel Black was located on the east side of the main road through Middle Sackville on the site now occupied by a filling station. About 1860, Joseph L. Black built a new store on the opposite side of the street. The new store was 80 feet long and had a 50 foot frontage
column2: it was a three-storey structure with a basement under the whole of it. At that time it was the largest mercantile establishment in Westmorland County. This building was superbly built and had been kept in excellent condition with very material additions added from time to time, until it was completely destroyed by fire last Sunday morning.

column1: The members of the firm before J.L. Black's death were: President, Joseph L. Black, Senator Frank B. Black and the late J. Walter Black. On the death of J. L. Black, Senator Black was elected president. After the death of J. Walter Black his two sons, on coming of age, were taken into the business. The present stockholders are: Senator Black, and his sons, J. Lawrence and Josiah William Black
column2: and the sons of the late J. Walter Black, Robert S. and John A. Black. The officers of the firm today are: President, Senator Black
column4: director, Robert S. Black and Secretary-treasurer, J. William Black.","Reprinted from the Saint John Telegraph Journal, Wednesday July 5, 1939","Black's Store

column1: 5292,object05292,record,,,,,Reminiscences of Sackville in 1854:A  Ship-Launching in Sackville,"Inch, James Robert 1835-1912",Allisonia|The White Fence,,,,Number 46,"1904, March
column4: The launching of a large vessel was always an event of great interest, not only to the people of the surrounding country, but especially to the teachers and students who occasionally were granted a half-holiday to watch the stirring scene. It was a great privilege, eagerly sought after but granted to but a few of the students, to be allowed on board
column5: for accidents occasionally occurred. I remember on one occasion when a large ship, after having a quick start, stuck upon the ways. After several attempts to move her, about thirty or forty men and boys, including some of the older (Mount Allison) Academy boys, were taken on deck where they were marshaled three abreast and the word of command ran with measured step the whole length of the vessel. On the second or third race the vibration caused by running set the great hulk in motion and the launch was successfully completed. Those who never witnessed a launch can form but a faint conception of the excitement aroused. The interest increased from moment to moment during the preparation. Men with heavy mauls were placed in order under the bilge of the vessel to drive the wedges which loosen the shores.","Reprinted from the Allisonia, Mount Allison Archives in The White Fence",Ships

column1: 5293,object05293,record,,,,,"Reminiscences of Sackville in 1854:  Arrived At Mount Allison On August 10, 1854","Inch, James Robert 1835-1912",Allisonia|The White Fence,,,,Number 46,"1904, March
column3: ,p.2,Periodical;Online,Ft Excerpt  :  ....The Post Office then in charge of the late Christopher Milner, was a small building about 12 x 16 which stood on the site of the present Chignecto Post Printing office. A little window with a swinging pane served the purpose of delivery. A shutter hinged on the top, when lifted into a horizontal position and supported by a brace - was the only protection, in all sorts of weather, which the patrons of the office enjoyed when waiting for their mail. How well I recall the noisy assemblages of academy boys waiting for their letters in the morning. The principal mails usually arrived at night. The breakfast hour at the academy was 7 o'clock winter and summer. After breakfast a crowd of boys would start for the Post Office which was supposed to be open at 8 AM. As the school-bell rang at 8:30 and the students were supposed to be in their school room at 8:45 there was but half an hour for obtaining the longed-for letters. The waiting minutes were occupied with all sorts of horse-play, interspersed with such shafts of wit and repartee as school boys are wont to fling at each other. But as the moments passed and no post master appeared, a few of the bolder ones would act as a deputation to pay a domiciliary visit to the tardy official for the purpose of hastening his advent. This usually had the desired effect, and Mr. Milner would be seen coming with hasty steps, grumbling good-naturedly and denouncing the impious rascals for disturbing him at his morning prayers.","Reprinted from the Allisonia, Mount Allison Archives, in The White Fence","Stage Coaches
column40: |http://heritage.tantramar.com/WFNewsletter_46.html,"Reminiscences of Sackville in 1854:  Arrived At Mount Allison On August 10, 1854","Inch, James Robert 1835-1912",,,Allisonia|The White Fence

column1: 5294,object05294,record,,,,,Reminiscences of Sackville in 1854:  A Trek Down East Main Street,"Inch, James Robert 1835-1912",Allisonia|The White Fence,,,,Number 46,"1904, March
column3: ,p.3,Periodical;Online,Ft Excerpt  :  On the main street leading to Upper Sackville there were probably less than half the number of residences which face that busy thoroughfare at present. A conspicuous and picturesque object was the large wind-mill which drove the lathes and saws in Silas Black's carriage shop ( corner of Ogden Mill and Main ). Its revolving fans glinting in the rays of the sun as the (Academy) boys took their walks in that direction always attracted attention. A little farther, on the left side of the road ( current site on Danny Doncaster's farm ), was the modest store in which Mariner Wood, the father of Senator Wood, laid the foundation upon which was built the prosperous business now conducted by the firm of M. Woods & Sons. A short distance beyond Mr. Wood's place of business stood a small harness and shoe shop, and a hundred yards farther a tannery. These have developed into the extensive works of the Standard Manufacturing Company and the widely known shoe manufacturing concerns of Abner Smith and son James. On the crest of the hill beyond, overlooking the fine expanse of water known at that time by the un-poetical name of Morice's Mill Pond, Joseph L. Black had just started business in a little building which stood nearly opposite his present fine store.","Reprinted from the Allisonia, Mount Allison Archives, in The White Fence","Main Street

column2: Full Text,"FT Excerpt:    Alderman Charles Fawcett, who on Thursday noon returned from his wedding trip, was that evening treated to a quadruple chivaree of unexpected tumultuousness. The employees of the Foundry, little boys from from all parts of Sackville, the members of the Sackville Band, and the men of the fire brigade successively exerted themselves to outdo one another in making night hideous.  It is reported  that it cost Mr. Fawcett between forty and fifty dollars to square things with his tormentors.",,"West Australian Church News

column1: 5312,object05312,record,,,,,The Bull and The Leaping-Pole,"Roberts, Charles G.D.",Around the Camp-Fire,,New York,Crowell,,1896,45-,Book:
column2: Full Text,"FT Excerpt:  Out on the Tantramar  marshes the wind, as usual, was racing with superfluous energy, bowing all one way the purple timothy tops, and rolling up long green waves of grass that shimmered like the sea under the steady aftenoon sun.  I revelled in the fresh and breezy loneliness, which nevertheless at times gave me a sort of thrill, as the boblinks, stopping their song for a moment, left no sound in my ears save the confused ""swish"" of the wind. Men at times talked of loneliness of the dark, but to my mind there is no more utter solitude than my be found in a broad white glare of sunshine.",,Marshes

column2: Full Text,"FT Excerpt:  Canada wants women cultured, refined, true, courageous, to tone down the crudities of our social customs, to show the women of our country that life consists of something better than endless rounds of social functions, to put the frown of their disfavour upon drinking customs that are sapping the young manhood of our land, to inform with their higher ideals and broader culture a so-called society that makes the petty amenities of life ""the be all and end all of existence"" to meet and teach and mould the crude alien millions who threaten to Europeanize or Orientalize our Canada, to make intelligent, patriotic citizens of the children in our public schools, to make bright wholesome happy Christian, Canadian homes and everywhere to refine and elevate and sweeten and purify social taste.",,"Closing Exercises

column1: ........Jeremiah Bancroft’s journal book in the expedition to the eastward in the company under Capt. Phenehas Osgood  ________________________________________________________________________
The tenth of April, the Regament appear’d at Boston and passed muster and
received thare cloths also the remainder of thair bounty and thaire wages from thare
inlesting to the fourtenth of April inclusive.
May ye 1. We had orders to sail and fall below Casel Wiliam anchored near
Deer Island. We lay for some days. The 3 of May, died a man belonging to Capt.
Perys company and was buryed on said island.
Sunday, May ye 11 being on ye quarter deck I had ye pleasure of seeing the
Snow pass by us which had our arms on bord. After that I went on shore and heard
Mr. Phillips preach from Luke 3 and 14.
Saturday, the 17th. following we had orders to be in rediness to sail ye first
Sunday the 18th. Parson Phillips preacht from the first of Timothy 4 and 8. Being
wind bound, we did not set sail till Thursday ye 22nd. We came to sail at 6 o’clock
in the morning and arrived in the Bason of Annapolis on Munday evening ye 26th.
with the fleet all safe which I desire to bless God for and hope He will stil prosper us
Wednesday ye 28th. It being acention we drank a helth to those we left at home.
Nothing more remarkable while in the Bason of Anapolis Royal but cursing and
swearing because they was dinyd thare alowence of rum.
Sunday ye 1 of June. We set sail for Forte Lawrence at Chignecto whare we arrived
on Munday ye 2nd. at Sd forte and on Wednesday the 4th. set out for the french
forte and having a bridge to lay over the river in order to go over to them opposit to
a block house whare the french had placet 6 canon also a _______ battery they
had and we marcht in Batalion form came to the place where we war met by a great
number of french and Indians who made a hideous yelling and then firing upon
us with thare canon and small arms. We had 4 field peaces which ware six poundes
which we or at least our guners got ready and returned thair shot as good as they
sent and much better, then a smart engagement began between the two party our
man fireing and husaing that within an half hour they set thar block house on fire
and several other buildings and our men furiously pushing forward with undaunted
courage that in an hour and a half we drove them of and took possession of thare
village. We set down to refresh ourselves, afterward we marcht a mile and campt.
Meanwhile the french returned to the forte and set thare mass house on fire and several  other buildings.  We had but one man killed that belonged to the regulars
and six or seven wounded though we ware all on the open march.39 Blessed be God
for granting us success thus far.
On Thursday, ye 8th, we marcht but a little way and halted cleared a rode
and built our camps about a mile and a quarter of the forte.
Friday ye 6th. Capt. Cob came up the river at which ye french fire 2 canon
and running down to the marsh, fireing with thare small armes intending to bord
him but they ware soon obliged to return by reason our men ran upon em fireing
also Capt. Cob fireing his swivel. We Judgd that we kild fore or five of em but have
recd no hurt from them also this even we had a skirmish with the french and indians
Saturday ye 7th, and soon we begin to get our canon to shore and clear a rode
acrost ye hill from ye marsh to ye main path in order to pitch our tents and hall our
artilery-- also this night ye indians fired at us but did us no hurt. We still continue to
get our artelery toward the forte.
Sunday ye 8th. the Indians touck ensign Hay who belonged to ye regulars also
our men tuck a frenchman. Also this day Col. Winslow went out with 400 men
and had an engagement with ye french Driving them into ye forte and soon after
returned. Afterward came a flagg of truce  in order to exchange prisoners which was
not agreed to. They say they will keep ye forte as long as they have powder and ball.
Tuesday ye 10, Col. Winslow being out with 340 men Discovred about 20 or 30
of ye enimy fred upon em and they returning a few guns fled.
Wednesday ye 11 Col. Winslow went out to ye hill whare they intent to entrench
the french fired several canon but hurt no one.
Thursday ye 11th.  Capt. Adams being out with 100 men 2 or 3 of
the and tuck one prisoner also came one of his own acord which deserted
Also Col. Scott56 went out with 300 men and with them ye regulars in order to
entrench the enimie being awaw of them came out and had placed themselves in
thare Lurking Places and as soon as our men came to ye hill ye enemy fired upon
them, a smart engagement soon begun between ye partys which lasted an hour and a
half and in ye skirmish they wounded Majr. Prebble in his [thigh which] affronted
so that  he ordered the men to run upon them which they perceiving nd
also seeing Capt. Adams with his company coming, fled to ye forte. They have kild
1 man and wounded five or six of which one was our chief guner.  We must in this
acknowledg the goodness of God who seems still to be on our side and smiles at our
Friday ye 13. We have 5 cochorns to Play upon the forte and have thrown 3 or
4 Bums into ye forte which we hope has dun some good execution  The french
has returned our shot but have not hurt no man this day except one that [h]ad his
carteridg box shot from his side and his britches cut on one thigh and his Baonet
Saturday ye 14th. This morning thare was very brisk fireing on Both Sides but
we have recd no Damage except one of our 8 inch morters was split by a canon ball
also a number of french and indians ware on ye marsh akilling catle which our men
spied and sent out 50 men which Drove them of an tuck ye ox they had kild and
Sunday [y]e 15th, Last night was very rainy till teno’clock this morning During
which time thar but 4 or 5 guns fired from either party and then they fired briskly.
This day we got our 13 inch morter and h[o]ve near 20 Bums of those the most of
which we think did good execution one in particular set a house on fire but they

column2: the french shot 143 canon and about 20 Bums at us this day but

column1: did (not) hurt any men and our men h[o]ve about 100 Bums small and great and
hope they have done good execution.
Munday ye 16th. After we had hove a few Bums the enemy sent a flagg of truse
to ye trench of Col Scott and Requested a cessation of arme for 48 hours but it

column2: whereupon they came to Col. Mungton to the campt but thare

column1: request was not granted by him but the Col demanded ye forte and told them that
if they did not give up soon he would hoist his Bloody Flagg and not take it down
till he had Destroyed them all and so gave two hours to Consider and mak Return.
Soon after the truce was gon the indians fired upon our centrays and the Bullets
flew among our tents the word was  given Stand to your arms which was Dun
as it seemed in a minute and we fired so furiously upon them that they emediately
withdrew and tuck to thare heals but we tuck one Indian that we wounded that
was calld Capt. John and many of ye English knew him he was mortelly wounded
but not past speaking and he informed us that we had killd several of them which

column2: the french hearing the engagement hurried away as fast as the

column1: could with another truce and note that Col Scott hearing ye engagement thought ye
truce was only a trick ordered all ye morter to be loaded and was aputing the match
too when Capt. Speakman standing on the bank of trench calld out a truce upon
which they forebor fireing and they came to Col Munkton and resigned ye forte
after which Col Scott marched in the first he being then at ye trench and at evening
Col Winslow with a party marcht in which caused great joy more especially to the
sick and lame. Note this night Capt John died at 10 oclk.
Tuesday ye 17th. Col Munkton named the Forte and the English flagg was Hoisted. also this night the french came from Gasperou at the Bay  of Vert and resighn the forte to the English.
Wednesday ye 18th. Col Winslow went to Gasperou to take Possession of that

column2: I went also myself.I found it to be a very Pleasant place and more so by our

column1: finding Sum Quantity of Brandy and wine for our Comforte.
We stayed till ye 23rd Instant then being relieved by Capt Speakman and 200
men we returned to our camp at Beausejoure.......",,"Winslow, John 1703-1774

column2: Full Text,"FT Excerpt:... Morton Hastings, caretaker of the rural cemetery had rather a bad quarter of an hour this afternoon. A man named Kennedy whose brother, now in the penitentiary, had married Hasting's younger daughter, accompanied by his wife and his wife's father , came over from Amherst and called upon Hastings.  Being loaded up with bad whiskey,   they soon became uproarious and joined battle with their host and his household.   Mayor Pickard who lives hard by the Hastings home, was sent for.   At this the marauders became alarmed, got aboard their carriage and started for Amherst. But they didn't thus ecape.   Mayor Pickard, reinforced by Marshall Sullivan and Bill C. Rayworth,  set out in pursuit and overtook them just before they entered Nova Scotia.   The three were brought back to Sackville and lodged in the lock-up and will be brought before Magistrate Jordan on Monday.",,"Andrews, Wilbur W.

column1: 5384,object05384,record,,,,,Sackville News: Success of Mount Allison Forward Plan,Smith Alfred Dean,Saint John Globe,,,,,"March 4, 1912",p.2,Microform,"FT: Excerpt

After the receipt of two vey indefinite and unsatidfactory cablegrams in the course of the last five or six weeks respecting Captain Ernest L. Anderson who was reported dangerously ill in the Falkland islands, Mrs. Anderson, his wife, got a despatch a day or two ago form Montevideo, S.A. stating that the Captain had been suffering from a severe attack of pneumonia, followed by a relapse, but had recovered sufficiently to start for home, and would be due in Sackville about March 25th.  During his last visit to Sackville he  was quite seriously ill of the same complaint. Capt Anderson comes from remarkably nautical stock. His grandfather Titus Anderson was a sea captain, and a good one too.  For of his sons were also captains of the first rank.  One of these, named George, had three sons, probably as good shipmasters as any of the stock, and Captain Ernest was the second of these sons.  Here in three generations we have eight first class navigators.   Where can this record be equalled . One of the second generation is Captain T.R. Anderson, one of our Aldermen from the West Ward, who guides the miniciple ship of state as skilfully as in former days he directed the course of his ocean craft.",newspaper

column1: The few Weeks preceeding the opening of College were undoubtedly the most eventfull in the history of our Empire. The act of a deranged schoolboy in far-off Sarajevo was made the pretext for a war while is now devestating Europe and causing great suffering to untold millions. The fact of conflict has been borne in upon us by the absence of at least three students who, in the ordinary course of events, would have been prominent members of our student body.""",,World War I

column97: Full Text,"FT: Excerpt: p. 62.   ""Every girl realises that this is a year to be faced seriously. The whole civilized world is plunged into a crisis so terrible that its like has never been known. Some of the best and most efficient of our men must go. But "" Business and usual"" is the slogan and it will  be in very many cases that  women must fill the vacant places. Will they be well prepared for this? This is the question that the Alpha Beta Society is answering, But it needs the support of everey girl in the University, the officers no matter how capable cannot do everything....""",,Alpha Beta Society

column4: At a recent meeting of the student body the subject of the establishment of a University Training Corps was discussed. Great interest was shown in the meeting and a resolution was unanimously passed expressing the desire of the students for the formations of such a corps.  It is to be regretted that steps were not taken before to attain this end.  Other college are, we are sorry to say, far ahead of us in the matter.  Dalhousie recently started an Officers Training Corps amid great enthusiasm and reports indicate that the  movement is meeting with the strongest support of both students and graduates.  U.N.B., McGill, Queens, and Toronto have also started military organizations and a large number of their students are now in training.  The announcement made a few days ago by our honored President leads us to hope that here at Mount Allison we may soon have a Training Corps.  We depreciate anything savouring of aggressive militarism or noisy ""jingoism"" ,  yet we cannot but feel that the great Empire, of which Candan forms no inconspicuous part, is engaged in a struggle which perhaps will demand the service of every able-bodied man before the drum is hushed and the cannon silent.  We pride ourselves upon the fact that we are a race that never acknowledges defeat and the resolution  which is strongest when the outlook is darkest.  Here at Mount Allsion we have a large body of men who have education and a good phsique.  If it is true that the universitites get the choicest of the young life in our country it follows that we have here the very material out of which officers can be made to lead the troops which Canada may yet contribute to the Empire's need.  The impression is gaining ground that the struggle now being waged in Europe is likely to be long and tedious.  Many of the students who would like to respond to the call of the Empire wish to finish the college year upon which they have entered.  A course of systematic training during the winter months would make such men valuable additions to a regiment in the spring.  Only a few days ago, a valued member of the Junior Class left us to take his place as an officer in the second contingent which Canada is about to furnish.    We hope that steps will speedily be taken to organize an Officers Training Corps and are confident that the proposition will meet with the approval of the entire student body.",,World War I

column1: The few Weeks preceeding the opening of College were undoubtedly the most eventfull in the history of our Empire. The act of a deranged schoolboy in far-off Sarajevo was made the pretext for a war while is now devestating Europe and causing great suffering to untold millions. The fact of conflict has been borne in upon us by the absence of at least three students who, in the ordinary course of events, would have been prominent members of our student body.""",,World War I

column4: Photographs,"FT excerpt p. 10, ROY BALMER LIDDY:     It would be pleasing at any time, but is particularly so in these days of hight patriotic feeling to welcome a professor whose education is a compleete Canadian product.  The Presence of such men as Dr. Liddy among us is an inspiration to the native student , who is beginning to feel it no longer necessary to obtain the stamp of a foreign university upon his dgree.  We look forward with pleasure to the day when the national pride in our educational facilities will be as strong as it already is in our commercial and industrial affairs, and the proudest boast of the scholar will be that his scholarship was ""made in Canada"".""",,"Faculty

column30: Full Text,"FT: Excerpt:     Never before in the history of our University, have such conditions obtained as are in evidence today.  The tramp of platoons and the word of command are heard on the campus, in place of clashing yells, and the referee's whistle.  The Residence, once crammed to  its doors, sends back a hollow echo from rooms whose former occupants  are ""somewhere in France,"" and the occasional hilarity of midnight gatherings is saddened  by the thoughts of those who might have been here.  Feeling the incongruity of engaging in public sports at such a time, where our old companions are suffering and dying for us
column31: feeling the selfishness of expending money on amusement when men of our blood are looking to us for the very necessities of exsistence, feeling that we who stay at home, should  at least do what we can, the students of Mount A. have this year withdrawn from Athletics.

column1: |  It seems almost incredible that such an action should have been misunderstood , whether willfully or otherwis, but such is the case. Mount Allison has been accused of fearing defeat, of not having enough men to for teams, and we know not what else.  We do not propose to consider the matters here, or to offer defence or explanation of Mount Allison's attitude, for such an attitude should need no defence in these days.   We can readily understand that other men may not see with us in the matter, we can readily understand that other Universities may conscientiously and honstly believe that some other course to be best.   We respect their opinion and bow to their criticism.   But we feel it to be a stigma and a disgrace upon Canadian manhood ,  and we share in the disgrace in so far as we are also Canadians, that any man can exist in our country who are unable to understand an action prompted by those feelings of patriotism and self-sacrifice which should  be   sufficiently common today.

We are running, this year, a new column in place of the Athletics column of former years Through volume after volume, the Argosy has followed the fortunes of Mt Allison Men in the games which they undertook
column2: and now that so many of those who were, or would have been, prominent in our records of sport and college activity are are players of the game of nations out on the fields of Europe, we would follow, and write of them still, for the honour of old Mt. A. . . . . . . . .",,"World War I

column33: Full Text,"FT: Excerpt:      . . . . . . . . Within the last few months, great changes have swept over our land.   The future is more uncertain that ever.  Many of our educated men are taking up arms in defence of our country.  Who is qualified to to take thteir place and carry on their work?  No one is more capable of doing this than the educated woman, - she who has been trained in a University, and particularly in its literary societies, where she has acquired poise, and mastered, to a certain extent at least, the art of public speaking.",,World War I

column119: Periodical,"FT: Excerpt:   There is at least one interesting feature for which we shall watch this year, and that is a larger proportion than usual of lady contributors to the college magazines.   If Mount A may be taken as typical, the young ladies of academic work are in as much evidence as ever and their numbers are as great as last year.  We imagine they are doing more letter writing and more knitting, and the imaginations of their hearts have a wider range than before the war began, but if we mistake not, they will also rise to this academic  occasions, and play a larger part than ever in relation to the university magazines.    If, as we venture to hint above, the college magazine is a keen interpreter of college life and spirit, our Exchanges will not lack the sweetness of a ""softer mouth"" and the ""big bow-wow strain"" of the over-maculine literary efforts of the halcyon years of peace.",,Editors

column203: Full Text,"FT: Excerpt:   In looking at the University Library, one is struck by the absence of a particular set of books, which might reasonably be expected in a prominent place. We refer to the bound volumes of the Argosy. . . . . Financial figures and numerical statistics may constitute  a sufficient history of a business, but the real history of a college lies far way from the realm of blue books and reports.  The traditions of a university are the traditions of its men, not of its commercial standing
column204: two thirds of its future lies in its past.   The maintenence of this history as set forth in its Argosies, is, what we submit, a matter of what should be given attention to by Faculty and Students alike. . . . . .  . . . We  are making history today, - not world history alone, but college history, of a type never before produced in the annals of our modern universities.  One only has to think of a few years ago, and think how absurd it would have appeard to us to think of the men we cheered on the football field, and chatted with in the halls, being wounded, being prisoners, dead, to realize what the college history of this war will mean to those students of days to come, how the records we pass over with a moment's anxiety, or a moment's rejoicing, will be to them as wonderful tales of an unbelievable age.  Conditions to us which are matters of grim reality, will be to them as fairy tales.  No football, a student body depleted to numbers pitifully small, the girls knitting socks at the meetings of our societies, and at public concerts , the constant emigration of recruits from among us, and over all, the subtle silent shadow of war, - unbelievable, absurd, they will seem.  In this matter we again make an appeal to our readers, - assist us to keep our columns filled with the news of our Mount A soldiers, for not only do such items interest the reader of today, but tomorrow thay will constitute the charter of the college in its place among the universities of the reconstructed world...The history of our men at the front, as Mount Allison men, will be found only in the Argosy. Let us strive to make that history as complete as possible. . . . .",,Editorials

Captain,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLII,December,1915",p. 174-179,,"FT:   Excerpt:    pp.177-178    On Monday we returned again to the Trenches, where we became a mark for German aeroplanes, which hovered over all day, dropping bombs, and smoke signals for the enemy artillary, so that the position was heavily shelled.   At five o'clock the order came to advance and to take an outwork lying in front of the trenches.  The ground over which we had to pass was completely swept by the enemy's machine guns, and it seemed like sure death to go ahead. However, we turned out, and some of us reached the advance  work in safety.   After we had been there a short time , the officer's decided the position was too exposed to hold, and ordered us to retreat as best we could.  Since it was so near dark, it seemed wiser to some of us toremain where we were than to cross again through that zone of machine gun fire, so another man and I, who were lying side by side, dug ourselves in and prepared to wait..  Meanwhile the German big guns had begun to find the position, and it soon became so warm where we were, that I decided it was better , after all, to risk getting back to the trenches.  I suggested this to the man with me, and he agreed, but he said ""Just a minute"".  He was lighting a cigarette, and I think he meant to say ""Just wait a minute till I light this cigarette."" but he never finished the remark, for at that moment a shell struck him, blew him to shreds, and buried me.  I managed to claw my way out, and started to run back, but as I went, my eyesight grew fainter and fainter and before I had gone a hundred yards, I was tone blind.   Our men passed me, on the advance, but could do nothing for me then so I was left lying helpless....",,"Chambers, Stanley

column197: Full Text,"FT:  Excerpt: Lieut. Carter, advancing with his machine gun sections, was struck in the hip by a fragment of red hot schrapnel casing, which tore down one leg, paralyzing him from the waist downward.  ""I lay there for a moment,""  he said ""and looking over my shoulder, I could see the blood spurting.  I could not use my legs, but I bean to claw my way toward our trench, pulling out handfuls of grass, digging into the earth, and pulling myself along by anything which could afford a grip.   When I neared the trench, Lieut Lawson caught sight of me, and scrabbling over, attended to my wound, using his first aid kit, and carried me back to the trench.  I lay in a dug-out for two or three hours, before I could be carried back.  The stretcher  bearers  use a big bag or hammock to carry you in, and after you have lain two or  theree hours, until your wound becomes cold and stiff, it is no joke to go twisting and turning around angles and curves in that thing.  Lawson, who helped me back into the trench, has been killed since I left.""",,"World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT Excerpt, p. 205-6:    At this season our thoughts naturally turn to the men of Mount Allison who have gone to fight.  The immanence of the struggle has been brought home to us more vividly than ever, by the contant out-going of recruits all through the past term, by the places filled today, and empty on the morrow, and by the few convalescent soldiers who we have been fortunate enough to entertain.   The response of the present student body to the call for men, has been prompt and large.  In the Argosy staff alone, as appointed last year, there are now four vacancies caused by the war, and the executive sections of the various societies have suffered alomost as heavily.  The YMCA has been obliged to re-appoint a president twice, and the Athletics and Students Council have each given their original chairman to the Army.  Of the Student Body generally, a percentage rather larger than three in every ten has enlisted since the Residence opened last September, - and this without any special unit or platoon, or section being raised from the University as an incentive to patriotism.   Although badly shattered by her losses, although left with a student body of only, approximately sixty-five men, although finding it hard to carry on even the ordinary affairs of routine, Mount Allison can hold her head high  among the colleges of her size, for she has done her share, and the measure of her service is not yet full.... The personal conviction and individual patriotism of these men, and of the numerous old students and graduates who have volunteered, cannot reflect credit upon any but themselves. We think of them first as soldiers fighting for their country.   But after all, deep in our inmost heart of hearts, we like best and most frequently to regard them as men of old Mt.A.. . . . . . . .",,"Editors

column18: Full Text,"FT: p.216:     As for me I am having the time of my life. All I do is get up at 6:30, have my meals and go to bed about 9 pm.   Once in a while I am very busy, but not very often.  The Doctor has been sick off and on lately and I have had to take his place to the best of my ability. Can you imagine me going around taking a fellow's temperature and feeling his pulse and prescribing for him?   Some doctor, eh?  Well that is what I have to do.    We are in the middle of winter season over here and believe me it is some winter.  Rain and hail every day.  I think we have had only three fine days during the last three weeks. Talk about mud. I thought Sackville was bad enough but it is ten times worse over here.   Really, without one word of a lie, it is up to your knees in our camps.   I was walking, or rather, trying to make my was through it one morning when I got stuck.   I could not move.   On trying to get out of it, I stumbled forward and both my boots came off and I was left stranded in the mud in my socks.   Instead of getting out of it, I got right into it.  I have a dug-out for a home.   There is an open fire place in it and when it is filled with soft coal, believe me the place is just like home. . . . . . Sergt. Harold Hanson,",,"World War I

column24: Full Text,"FT: excerpt p.253        I  was touched by the little Mount Allison box I received at Christmas and especially when I found it contained a package of cigarettes.   Dear old Mount Allison, you don't believe in cigarettes at all but you still love your boys so well that you pander to their minor vices.  . . . . . . In recent years I have often reflected with wonder on what Mount Allison did for us poor ministers' sons who arrived in Sackville with one suit to our backs and with scarcely a penny in our pockets.... . . . Frank Parker Day, '03.

column1: . . . . .Just a  note to tell you my Christmas box arrived in good condition on Christmas eve.   I was delighted on opening the package to find it contained ""Greetings from Old Mt A."" and there was apicture of the old campus that I love so well.  My eyes filled at the sight of it, and my heart warmed as I read, "" Your Alma Mater sends this memento"" and ""Remember Old Mt A.""  It seemed  so good to have the old college remember one. . . . . It is encouraging and cheering to know that while we are out here trying to do our bit we are not forgotten by those whose duty keeps them in the home land.   You can always depend on our standing true to our colours.   The Garnet and Gold will never be disgraced by the men who represent it.  We  learned how to play a clean game while at Mount A. and we promise to ""play the game"" to a finish here.

column21: Portrait,"FT:  Excerpt, P. 266.   It is with feelings of more than mere duty that we endeavour to gather for our College Magazine all items relating to our university and her place int he war.   We have already spoken in these columns of the adviseablility, even the necessity of making an keeping as  complete a record as possible of the events transpiring in these stirring times which have any relation to the history of the college.   We would impress once more upon our readers the importance of supplying the Argosy with news of this description.   And we would suggest again to the students, the formation and arranging of a collection of suitable momentoes of these days which will be of incaluable value to the students of the future.   We feel, as we have already suggested, something of the never dying thrill that they will always awaken, as we edit these soldiers' stories we are fortunate enough to obtain.   Long after our day has passed, when ourselves and our interests of the moment have become things of the hidden past, there will still lie within these pages, words which will stir the blood and fire the eye of collegiate generations yet unknown.    On these sheets now crisp and white enough, then tinged with the indefinable touch of time, the historian of the future will find material for his grandest chapters and his most noteable periods.  At least, he will find it if we get it.   The price being paid by Mount Allison men for these stories is high enough, and no research too laborious, or no information too complete to fulfill the duty we owe to the men who are fighting and to the college whose honor is theirs. . . . . . .",,World War I

column84: Full Text,"FT:p. 285, FRASER  "" We (the Company) are in supports now, while the remainder of the Battalion is in the front line. The companies take turns at this and it is more in the nature of a rest than any place we go.  The supports at this point consist of a series of dugouts. As they are exposed to the enemy trench, no fires are allowed in the day time, and the first sign of smoke brings a German whizz-bang which they are not slow to send over.  No one is allowed outside his dugout during daylight, so we put in our time sleeping. . . . . Just received a parcel of tinned stuff from the Mount A.  bunch in China through Chis Graham.  The old spirit again.

column1: p. 287 MACDOUGALL:   We have been quiet for a long time, shooting a little every day, but we are looking forward to a spring move.  The weather has been remarkably mild. Mud is an ever present scourge. . . . .  .   I received a Christmas present from Dicky Dickinson, Mildred Armstrong, and Harry Irish (West China).  Surely great things can be done in the name of old Mount A.

p. 287 COCHRANE, C.H. I do not know to whom I am indebted for the little Xmas. box from old Mt. A. but I am indeed greatful, and I think that I am expressing the thoughts of every Mount Allison man who is at present serving his country at the front, when I say that the little gift was truely appreciated. . . . We in the cavalry, are fairly comfortable compared to the poor chaps in the trenches who, in many cases are up to their waists in mud and water. Believe me, I take off my hat to the man in the trenches every time.

p. 289.  MERSEREAU:  I want to thank Mt A. again, through you, for the lovely little parcel sent to her boys over here. It was such a delightful surprise, words cannot express the feelings it gave me.  Had a lump in my throat and almost acted the baby when I opened the parcel and read the note. . . . .You know at the front it is not at all like you imagine.  Things come as a matter of course, you haven't hardly any time to think.   The days are quiet enough and nearly all the work is done from dusk to daybreak.  The first time under fire you are a little afraid as the bullets go by close to you, but you get so  you never mind them in the least. But the shells are Hell.  Anyone who says they do not fear shell-fire is a liar.   You crouch into the smallest possible position and pray that the won't hit you.   The noise is something awful when they explode, and one can feel the air being simply torn asunder.   Of course one learns to hug the ground pretty closely, and you don't mind mud or anything, just get as close to nature as the Lord will let you.   Was out wiring one night in ""no man's land"" beween the trenches when a machine gun was turned on us.   Wish you could have seen how close I got to the earth.  - - 'twould astonish you. . . . . .",,"World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT: This is such a lovely day that I am sitting outside the dug-out, writing - the first good day we have had in some time.  We have been very busy lately, going from one trench to another - we were up and relieved the 19th Battalion when the British took the International trenches again.   It was an awful bombardment from both sides, and I must say my nerves were gone completely.   The German trenches were no more when our guns were completely through with them.   We  expected to get the word to change any minute, but the Gordons, R. Scots and Seaforths had no trouble taking them back.    They call them the International Trenches because they have changed hands so often.     It is quite peaceful here today, except the aerial guns are bombarding  away at our ships, but have not brought one down yet, and have wasted a lot of dollars trying.    We were down to La Clyte for a while, and and held the line a little advanced from where the Princess Pats made their last stand, and believe me it was a dirty corner, but I got out without a blemish.    That Verdun battle was a corker - terrible wasn't it?    They came 20 deep, a target the French gunners  had never hoped for, and their 75s surely did the work - without a doubt the greatest artillary fire the world has ever seen, they throw 23 shells a minute.   They must be wonderful.   Would like very much to see one,  and maybe I will get the chance before long, but we expect to be home before August - that is the talk around this Hell hole.

column1: We relieved the R.C.Rs this time, and got our old lines back. We hope to get our passes soon, and if we do, why I am going to spend some time in Glasgow Scotland, as I have been invited up by one of my boys.   They pay our way to wherever we go in England, but won't let us go to Paris - I would love to see Paris, and will never get another chance, not likely, but no use!    There are so many Canadians over here they have a hard time finding Billets for them, and still they come, and still we get no rest.   We need one badly, we have been in every other week since September 20th, and lately we are in and out every three or four days, but that cannot last because the men cannot stand it.    We had quite a lot of snow last week, and hill 67 is covered with it yet. - It is up behind us and commands this part of the country.  Mt Kemmel is the correct name of it.    I am bayoneting man with B section, and hope to get a big Hun on my bayonet some day soon.   It is not much fun shooting at them.  Well, hoping to see you all some time in July, I will leave you for this time.
Amen - Tim       P.S. Never was better in my life",,"World War I

column1: 5636,object05636,record,,,,,A Mt. A. Nurse at the Front,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLII,March 1916,pp. 375-6,Microform
column4: [In reply to a gift of $20 from MtA's Red Cross Society]        I have been putting off answering your letter, hoping for an uninterrupted time when I could compose my thoughts.  However, such times do not come to us ""somewhere in France"".    Please accept for yourself and extend to every single Allisonian who helped with your lovely gift my sincere thanks on behalf of No. 2 Canadian Stationary.   If you could know the many little extra comforts it has meant to our wonderful men, you would all feel repaid a thousand times for the  effort it meant.   It came too late for Christmas and New Years but has done its ""bit"" just the same.  The last of it went for a wee Canadian lad who had come to the end of his endurance - a nervous wreck - and we used part of your money to give him a birthday party yesterday.   He cannot sleep for hideous dreams , and his bed had to be moved so he could not see the hills in the distance.   He(a signaller) had watched hills from his post,   with no one to speak to, for so long, that he could just not see another hill.    It was a lovely birthday party - and he was happy.   The sisters spend so much - too much of their own money on such things, that it was wonderful to have gifts like yours to fall back on.      Now the way I am going to thank you for this gift, is to ask for something more.    Will you and some of your girls take an afternoon off some nice fall day, and get me some choice Maple leaves, press them and send them to me for our next Christmas decorations.    There was such a demand for them last Christmas, and we hadn't nearly enough. If you want to see a really enthusiastic Canadian - see him in France - on July 1, or Xmas day!     Our last concert party began their program with a quartette "" O Canada"" I wish you could have heard our Canadian ""Blesse et malade"" stamp and clap!    So don't forget my maple leaves for Xmas decorations.    We are expecting a rush any time now.   Heaven forbid it be a repetition of last spring.    We are adding 200 beds to our establishment. A bit awkward as we are cramped for space.   I'll have to put my next sisters in French billets which is quite deadly.    Our quarters are full to the brim now, and no place to pitch tents for sisters.

column1: How far away - part of another world - you seem - home seems!   Sometimes I wonder if I will ever see it again. Annie Strong,  Class of 1907.",,"World War I

column2: Periodicals,"FT Excerpt:  But the students of England do not stand alone as the inheritors of glory.   The younger sons, from the universities of Australia, of South Africa, and of Canada, have crossed the seas, to take their stand beside their English brothers on the battlefields of France.  Of Mount Allison's splendid contribution, I need not speak. Every echo that sounds through the deserted halls of this college is also an echo of glory, and every wind that blows over our quiet campus, whispers the names of the Mount Allison men who have answered their country's call to arms.   The relations existing between Mount Allison and these splendid sons is a reciprocal one.  This university honours them and they in turn remain true to their alma mater. The  indescribable something called college spirit never fails among Mount A men, even on the fields of Flanders its influence still lives.  And it is chiefly due to the members of our faculty that this college enthusism is due.  .  .  .  .",,"Valedictorians

5659,object05659,record,,,,,Commencement at Mount Allison,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLII,May 1916,p 499-518,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT: pp. 510-511 Convocation (Dr. Borden):  The outstanding feature of our university work for the past year is the same as that of almost every business enterprise in Christendom.   The dominant thought and interest of both professors and students have centered in the Great War.   Although a number of our students enlisted last year and still more joined the colours during the summer vacation, we started our classes last September with a fair attendance and during the year we have had a total registration of 199.    A ringing appeal for recuits came to us in October last, and the painful process of heart-searching began, until the whole student body was shaken to its depths.   One group after another enlisted, until 56 of the students who began work with us last September were gone.   From the 140 who were with us last year, and who would under normal conditions be with us now, over 80 have answered the call to arms.   I am satified that our Honor Roll, embracing as it does nearly sixty percent of our whole student body, contains a larger proprtion of recuits than any other college in Canada.   We have compiled a partial list of the Mount Allison men of all years who have joined the colours, which number must represent a creditable proportion of of our total past attendance.   In fact practically all of our undergraduate students who are qualified to enlist are already either under orders  or have offered to enlist....... [Rev. Dr. Byron Crane Borden]",,"Class of 1916

Armstrong,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,November 1916,p.11,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT Excerpt:  "" the fighting is now about the worst ever.  The artillary is simply awful.  It cannot be decsribed.  To watch a general barrage at night is wonderful and terrible.  The flashes of the guns run from left to right and back continuously without a break like lightening playing on a line of clouds and the noise of the shells passing is like the singing of a million telegraph wires.   The effect of such a bombardment is fearful. What were once woods become patches of shattered stumps.  Towns are mere piles of debris.  On town that is famous in war history consists of a piece of brick wall and two mine craters.******************It was a bit tough when we first went under fire and I shan't soon forget the experience of having a man killed alongside me.   Except for a general bombardment machine guns are worse.  One never gets used to these.   The gas worries me a good deal too. It is awful but we have good protection now if we are always on the alert.  . . . .",,"Armstrong, R. McL. Lieut.  (Mike)

5685,object05685,record,,,,,1920 Attention!,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,November 1916,p.18,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT: Excerpt p. 18-     .         The best message that can be given the men who are entering Mount Allison today is the message of the lives and deaths of Motyer and Boone and Elderkin and McKay and Welch - all Allisonians who sleep in Canada's sacred ground in France - and Colin Gray who gave his life for Canada before his battalion crossed to Europe. . . . . . . . . .  What manner of boys were these whose memories this university will always honour? . .  . . . . . . . . . . . . . They set in college days  examples which the students of the present may folllow in confidence that the path is straight.  . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Look up the class lists. The names of these boys are among the prize winners.   Sometimes too, their names are nearer the bottom of the class list than the top. . . . . .The faces of these boys look out from pictures of athletic teams which hang about the college halls. . . .Every one of them loved clean sport.  The Mount Allison of today has none of the spirited athletic contests of the years before 1914. . . . .. These boys who have died were loyal to Mount Allison.   They were students in times when there were strong class rivalries such as the Mount  Allison of today does not know.   They were ardent ""class men""
column90: but put the issue of class and college before them and they were ""Mount Allison men"" always.   When they had gone out from Mount Allison they knew their debt to her.   No student ever pays her in full with money, nor can pay.   These boys gave her a warm and constant affection.  I have heard most of them speak of that love, though all did not call it love perhaps eachin his own way spoke with a sincerity which I who knew him could not doubt.    . . . . . .Too often the newcomer to college misses the fact that in a college community, a small community, his whole life is constantly under observation by his fellows in the community and that if he is to influence them for good it must be by actions rather than words.  . . . The Mount Allison men who have fallen gave their lives in answer to the King's call for service, and a summons to service is the call which greets the men who come to Mount Allison for the first time this year for all men cannot fight but all can serve.*********************The world's  standards are being reforged in the white heat of war.    When peace has come service for self will weigh less in the balance which weigh'd men's worth, and service for others will more than ever be the unit of measurement.  . .. .. . . .",,World War I

5713,object05713,record,,,,,Locals,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,November 1916,p. 58-63,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT Excerpt p. 61   ""   Now as never before are the eyes of the world turned upon the woman - what is she going to do with the new opportunities for service which are opening before her?   Woman's sphere is no longer narrow and limited.  Twentieth century woman is proving the worth and practicability of the Single Standard.   The world that we will have to work in will be a man-made world, it is true, but it will be a world ruled on another principle than the old-time groundless distinction of sex.    In our Alpha Beta Society every attempt is being made to stimulate that broadening of interest and cultivation of free thought and speech which make for citizenship.   Debating has been given a more prominent place than ever before in our literary sessions  and a joint session with the Eurhetorian Society has been arranged for every third week.  This is, we feel, a move in the right direction - the sooner that we establish a feeling of perfect equality with men on the public platform the sooner will be our aim, as a socity be reached.""     P. 63     A meeting was held on Saturday evening September 30th, to reorganize the Red Cross Society of Mount Allison.   The officers and committees for the year were elected.   Of the many activities of our College, World Red Cross appeals most strongly in these terrible days.   We, who stay behind and are defended by the heroes multitudes that have left, we must not fail them, we must do our bit.   Girls, we want your help in the Red Cross.""",,"World War I

5714,object05714,record,,,,,Overseas Column,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,November 1916,p. 65 - 68,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:  p. 67       from J.D. MacLean 3rd Pioneer Battalion. 6 October, 1916.     "" Our old outfit was busted.  I left on the 25th of May bound for the Canadian Pioneer Training Depot. I was about the only sub. available at the time, so I went to France almost Immediately.   I sailed for Boulogne on the 17th of June and reported to this outfit on the 18th.   I only just missed the 3rd Battle of Wipers and ever since have been devoutly thankful that I did miss it.     Before leaving Folksone I saw Hen Lewis and Byron Winsor.    They left for France the same day I did but went via Southampton.   I haven't see any of that crowd since.    Norman Wetmore ('17),  Gerry Anglin ('18), Lyman Anglin ('18) and Lloyd Taylor ('18), came over  about the last of July.  Gerry went to the 26th, Norman to the 25th, Lyman to the 21st, and I don't know what became of Lloyd.   In the last scrap Norman got a bad one in the head
column61: his left side was paralyzed.   I think he will be all right soon enough, though.   Gerry managed to come through everything  so far.   How he did it I don't know, for they hace had a very warm time.       Murphy Pickup was lucky enough to raise a blighty (leave to England) after being here two weeks.   I saw him just before that but not since.   The other night, up the line, I was doing some work for a Mr.Smith of the Corps. Signals.  He was none other than Grant's brother who used to come up from Halifax every once in so often.    He told me that Grant has gone into  the Mechanical Transport end of the A.S.C.     Glad to hear that T.A. ( McDonald) , Billy West, Kruger McAfee, and the other chaps who went into the Siege Battery are not far from here.    They came over just lately, but I haven't located them yet.    Do you remember Clark, '08?   used to be a theologue, sometime a platoon officer in the 64th, later Chaplain to the 24th Pioneers and now with the 4th Division. I saw him about a month ago.    He was thriving and was liked very much wherever he went.      I haven't see much of the Mount A. bunch   over here
column62: We never seem to jibe somehow.   I did see  Paddeo though (R.W. Bartlett '17). We  had some time, believe me.   He is now in an Imperial Tunneling Company. Herder (Ralph Herder, '17) got his commision and I think, as an extra, a blighty.   He saw Perce Appleby on some isle in the Medditerranean. Paddeo's address in case you should ever want to write him is 177th Tunneling Co. B.E.F, France.""",,"World War I

5715,object05715,record,,,,,Exchanges,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,November 1916,p. 69,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:  p. 69:  .... . . . . . ."" If there is one thing which we shall endeavour to keep before us throughout the year, it is the fact that all axchanges  which come to us  are being published under the greatest difficulties, both financial and otherwise.    The heavy drain of the war upon the best and brightest of the young men of our Canadian universities must be reflected to a greater or lesser degree in the  literaray productions of our college magazines.    Not only is this true, but the many and varied dutues which thus necessarily devolve upon those who remain, render it practically impossible for even them to give to their paper the same time and energy that they were accustomed to bestow upon it in the past.  . . . . . . . . .",,World War I

5723,object05723,record,,,,,News from the Front,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,December 1916,p.84,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT
column274: Excerpt,p. 86:    If one could only raise some pity over here, it would be hard not to pity the poor mortals that have to stand up against our artillary down there.    I had the pleasure  one afternoon, of standing in a trench from which a battalion was going to advance to the attack in a short time.   I might say here that our own Battalion had already gone over twice and in staying in this trench we had to take all of our friend Fritz's spite in the shape of Artillary fire. . . . . .Imagine yourself under a huge fire hose and at a certain time, right to the second, the water sailed over your head with a swish. Such a noise does our barrage make when it opens up.   Every gun that is firing opens right on the dot.   Then of course a yell and a scamble of bayonets and men over the top follows as a matter of course, and away they go as though chasing a big steam roller, the barrage ahead of them......"" , G.G. ANGLIN . . . . . .      pp. 87-88. . . . . . . . "" Then I went, had tea and afterwards went into the Coliseum, one of the biggest theatres in London.. . . . I went in the Grand Tier and paid a shilling and saw as much as the lords and dukes in the boxes.  I only stayed in there about an hour and a half as I had a fit of nerves and got to shaking and trembling and had to get out.  Don't know what it is but nearly everyone has it on the front. Just the strain, I expect.   The music was very soft and low and worked on my nerves..... . . .. . G. G. HECKBERT",,"World War I

5731,object05731,record,,,,,Personalia,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,December 1916,p.112-115,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT p. 112:      There was an attendance of aout fifty at a delightful reunion of graduates of Mount Allison University, and others connected in one way or another with that noted Canadian seat of learning , at the residence, last evening, of Professor and Mrs. Lawrence Killam, at Kerrisdale.   Especial interest was attached to the reading of letters containing greetings and cordial good wishes from the former president, Dr. Allison:  from the present head of the university, Dr. Borden
column16: and from Prefessors Tweedie and Hunton,  members of the Faculty.  Much merriment was occcasioned by a grand auction at which a number fof trifles brought by the guests were bid up to exhorbitant sums, falling at the auctioneer's hammer to swell a sum which is to be dispatched forthwith to Miss Deinstadt, a graduate of the university, to be expended in purchasing cheer and little necessaries for patients in her charge at the French Hospital, near Verdun, in which she is busy at her work of mercy, nursing the soldiers back to health.  Letters were also drawn up to be forwarded on behalf of the company to be read at the university and to be posted and subsequently handed around among the large number of alumni now in the thick of the fighting  on the front.   Professor Killam, the host, a graduate of Mount Allison,    is connected with the department of physics at the Provincial University.......",,"World War I

5733,object05733,record,,,,,Overseas Column,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,December 1916,p.121-124,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:   from the St. John Globe   ""  Captain North came up from the mining shaft one day and found his trench being attacked by a bombing party.  The officers in charge had lost their heads. Most had disappeared.   Major North rallied the infantry, collected his engineers, and managed to repulse the attack.   There was a staff enquiry into the affair and the captain was recommended for the Military Cross, although it was not the first time he had earned it. . . . . . . .During the first battle of Ypres, young North took a prominent part in the building of a bridge across the Yser Canal under fire.  The  first two times the attempt was made,  it proved unsuccessful on account of spies giving away the position to the German artillary, and the losses were heavy,  but the third attempt was crowned with success. . . . . At another time when the Germans had shelled and knocked down the parapet of a trench occupied by the Gordon Highlanders so they had to leave that particular point, the Royal Engineers mining to get at the trench 30 yards distant, had also to withdraw their men from the undersground, leaving the workings unmanned.   There was great danger that the Germans, who were also mining, would break through.   Lieut. B. and young North, who were in charge of the engineers, decided to go back.   So they crawled past the broken parapets and went underground,  lighting their  way  with electric flashlights.  B. was about three feet ahead when they got to the face of one of the tunnels.   A German was there and fired at the lieutenant, but he missed,.   Then he dropped his weapon and ran.  North, armed with a revovlver, stayed in the tunnel on guard while B. went back for gun cotton, and when he returned they blew up the whole works..............",,"World War I
