column3: Full Text,"FT:We have been impresed again this year at college,  as in years past,  that the students do not  fully realise the value of the records which are at present growing under their hands.   Sp far as we know,  the volumes of the Argosy for the past two or three years have not been bound and files away with the volumes of other years.  So far as we know, no attempt is being made among the members of the student bosy to preserve and record of conditions in and around the college in these years . . . . . . . It is hard for the student to realize that the life they are iving today is not the normal college life. but the fact remains that it is quite the reverse.   It is a peculiarity of the mind that after living in quiet halls with a campus practically unused by foorball and track teams for two or three years, we should come to think of the halls as being normally quiet and deserted and the campus as  being normally a well prepared piece of ground  practically falling into disuse. . . . . . . . One of the greatest things which appeals to the visitor at any college is the history of its past. . . . . . . . . . . . .Mount Allison has won an enviable position among the colleges of the Maritime Provinces during the progress of the war, she should maintain that position after the war by preserving complete and detailed records. . . . . . . Let the students keep in mind that practicaly nothing is normal about college life today and let them form some association or combination to gather and preserve such records as we have suggested.",,World War I

When on Guard, that shalt challenge all parties approaching thee.    2.  Though shalt not send any engravings nor any likeness of any airship in heaven nor any submarine in the sea, for I, the censor, am a jealous censor, visiting the iniquities of the offenders with three months CB, but allowing mercy unto thousands by letting their letters go first who obey my commandments.    3.  Thou shalt not use profane language , unless under extraordinary circumstances, such as seeing thy mate get shot, or getting petrol in thy tea.    4.  Remember the soldier's week consists of seven days.  Six days shalt thou labor, and on the seventh thou shalt do odd jobs.   5.   Honor thy King and Country
column37: keep thy rifle well oiled and shoot straight that thy days may be long in the land the enemy giveth thee.   6.  Thou shalt not steal thy comrade's kit.   7.  Thou shalt not kill time.   8  Thy shalt not adulterate thy mess tin by using it as a shaving mug.   9. Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy comrade, but preserve silence on their outgoings and incomings.   10.  Thou shalt not covet thy corporal's post not the sergeant-major's, but by thy duty and perseverence, though shalt rise to the position of field marshall.",,World War I

5803,object05803,record,,,,,Commencement at Mount Allison,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,June  1917,p. 436-7,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:P. 443:  ACADEMY cLOSING,DR. PALMER  . . . The closing exercises of the Mount Allison Academy were held in the afternoon.   Dr. Palmer presiding.   Dr Palmer in his report, stated that the attendance this year was smaller then usual, owing to the prevailing conditions.   The total enrollment for the year was one hundred and four students.   Out of a total enlistment of one hundred and sventy-seven students for overseas service, one hundred and eight were exclusivley Academy students.   Several of the Academy students and teachers were present in uniform. . . . . . .. P. 447: Dr. BORDEN . . . . . The stress of war conditions has not affected this department of our work as it has that of the University and the Academy.   Notwithstanding all the strain and uncertainty and the increased cost of living that have characterized the last three years,  there has evidently been a growing appreciation  of the importance of female education and an increased confidence of the aadvantages offered by our Mount Allison Ladies College to meet the needs of the time...It wiLl be very evident from the essay on the war and prohibition to which you have just listened, that our students are encouraged to look beyond their textbooks and are disposed to take themselves in line with the great problems that the nations are facing today. . . . . . . UNIVERSITY rEPORTp. 453    If our ranks are being depleted by the death of strong, brilliant men who are thus being sacrificed, there is all the more reason what those of us who remain at home should brace ourselves to unremitting toil and self sacrifice.   Our best will be needed  by our Canada in the strenuous days of reconstruction that are even now upon us.   That our Canada shall be filled for a generation with broken men seems inevitable.   While the war will doubtless give back to our country thousands of splendid men who have been tried in the furnace and tested in such infernos of shot and hell as Ypres and Courcelet and Vimy Ridge, there will be broken fragments of shattered manhood which will demand the patient care and unceasing toil of our women and self sacrificing men.   Even today shiploads of wounded soldiers are being landed on our shores and the imperative call of the hungering peoples of the world for food and material is getting more and more shrill as the days go by.   Our students, bth male and female, who have been faithfully preparing mental soil for the seeds of truth, are now going out to plough and sow for a material harvest to help satisfy this world hunger. . . BYRON CRANE BORDEN . . . . . . . . .P.456:   You are going forth from your Alma Mater today but a remnant of the goodly class that entered Mount Allison in 1913.   I have no doubt that you would all be glad to render the same service to your country that your fellow students are rendering, if you had been permitted.   I am sure you will go out and serve.   We know the spirit that prompts you.   We have tried you at every turn  and have never found any one of you wanting.    . . . . . B.C. BORDEN   . . . . .  p. 456:An interesting feature of the evening was the conferring of the degree of Music Bachelor upon Miss Eleanor Tait of St. John's Newfoundland.",,"Plays

5806,object05806,record,,,,,Overseas Column,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIII,June  1917,p. 465-6,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:   . . . . . .(VIMY RIDGE)  Easter Sunday we moved up to a place quite near the line, I should say as near as possible in daylight which was about five miles back. Here we bivouced all day. . . . . .During the latter part of the afternoon extra ammunition was issued , together with picks and shovels, flares, bombs, etc. . . . . My company finally reached its alloted trench about 12 midnight. Then came the long wait until the hour came for us to go over.    Promptly at 5:30 the barrage opened up and over we went,  following it.   No wonder the Hun beat it when he saw the hordes that were at him.    As we were going over his front line, we could see him running. . . . . .We wandered on and gained out first objective 35 minutes after the start.   The Ridge was practically ours four hours afterthe start.   Then came the hard part.   The weather was rotten, rain and snow all the time. Have no dug outs, so it was the open air treatment . . . . . Our platoon was ordered to go out to form a sort of an outpost with other platoons on the flanks.   I spent that night out digging in,   At about four am we were ordered to take uo another position,   preparatory to an  advance.    Our battalion advanced across the open that morning at daylight. . . . . .We made about one and a half miles that morning before we dug in.   This new trench we dug was called the New Brunswick trench. . . .  .I got a slilight scratch across the back of my hand, so slight you can hardly see it.   A shell burst on one side of the road, luckily I was on the other.  When we got back, Doc sent me to the dressing station to get some serum injected. . . . . . . . ARMSTRONG, R. McL. Lieut.  (Mike)",,"World War I

5812,object05812,record,,,,,Locals,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIV,November  1917,p.33,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:  THE UNIVERSITY has reopened and, in consideration of the fact that the war has enrolled many of our best manhood, it is gratifying to know that we have a fairly large and capable student body . . . . . . LADIES COLLEGE . . . It is not only in England that girls are doing the work of men, but also in Sackville, as was indicated last week when the ""potato-picking corps"" wended their way farmward.  To many it was new work, but in such large niumbers did the girls volunteer,  that Mr. Wigle feared more potatoes would have to be planted to meet the demand. . . . .",,Enrolment

5813,object05813,record,,,,,Overseas column,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIV,November  1917,pp. 37-40.,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT:  p. 40  I WAS NOT INJURED IN THE RAMSGATE AIR RAID, but I saw it. The ten big Gothas were a  fine sight.  I saw two of them shot down.  They seemed to be searching for the military hospitals.   Two bombs fell in the grounds of the Princess Pats and three hit the beach just below the Granville where I was.   They made two direct hits on Chatham House and Townley Castle where I had lived until I went over to the Granville for an operation.   Our losses were, two killed and about a dozen injured., some seriously.   On one of the wrecked air planes, maps were found indicating accurately the positions of the hospitals, so tha authorities  proceeded to get away from these at once.  With some others I was sent to Warrington for a time and thence down here.   . . . . . . .I was operated on by Col Stan, formerly head of Orthopedic Surgery at Toronto University, so I presume it would be hard to find a better man, he fastened the several ends of the alnot and median nerves, had to work up from the arm-pits, a deicdedly delicate operation.   They told me it would take about two years for my hand to come back. . . . . . LAURIE M. COLPITTS",,"World War I

column16: Full Text,"FT: p. 123   SINCE THE TIME WHEN Defoe penned his first editorial remarks probably no task more unique or pleasant has devolved upon the editor of a journal, than falls upon us this month, as it falls upon the editors of other college magazines, than that of welcoming to their Alma Mater men who have been overseas and who now, returned unfit for service, have directed their steps to the scene of their former struggles and are completing the courses entered upon before the gigantic conflict raging in Europe enlisted their services.    . . . . .",,World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT: p. 126   AS WITH EVERYTHING THE SOCIETY WAS HIT with a staggering blow by the war which, through voluntary enlistment, reduced its membership to one third its former size, but yet, it is very much alive and striving to makeup in energy and enthusiasm what it lacks in numbers.  Nine of its members have already been reported as having made the supreme sacrifice for the cause of right, other have been severely wounded, one is reported missing, two have been especially distinguished for valour on the field, and the end is not yet.... . . . .",,World War I

5841,object05841,record,,,,,News of Battle,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIV,February 1918,p. 154-161,Microform;Periodicals;Full Text,FT p. 160-161   THIS IS ONLY OUR SECOND DAY OUT, so I haven't had much chance to get anything done yet.   I find I owe so many letters, I'm rather ashamed to answer them.   I had to write the finacee of one of my men who was killed.  It is awfully tough writing those letters, though I ought to be used to them I suppose.   I didn't even have time Christmas day to wonder what you were doing.   Christmas eve and morning I spent putting up barb wire to keep the nasty Hun from getting too familiar with us   It was a beautiful moonlit night.   Toward morning the moon went down and it started to get dark.   One of the men got tangled up in the wire and got considerably peeved, and started saying things.   I reached over and wished him a merry christmas, it being the first time I thought of it.  He and I started laughing which annoyed the Boche, so we had to kiss the dirt tout de suite.   .  .   . . . . . . . I'm so sleepy tonight I don't know what I am writing . . . . .",,"World War I

5845,object05845,record,,,,,Overseas Column,,Argosy,,,,Vol. XLIV,February 1918,p. 183-187,,FT:  p. 184  [GERALD ANGLIN'S] BROTHER WROTE THE FOLLOWING TO HIS MOTHER:   In case he is too modest to send you many details I shall inclose now what I know.   Out of the 135 men he took over the top in ""B"" Company only 33 have come out and Bob Armstrong is missing.   Gerald was the last company CO to come out, the other two being wounded and the fourth killed.   As each went down he took command of their companies and job
column15: the Battalion dug in on their own objective.   Gerald's company was the first and only Company to actually reach the objective during the fight for the ridge.   And finally he probably got engrossed in his big job and a momentary exposure  brought his several machine gun bullets in is leg.   The two bones are badly chewed up as he says, and it is going to mean a long period of convalescence. LYMAN ANGLIN",,"World War I

column20: Full Text,"FT: p. 211 DONALD B. SMITH.  "" tHE FOOD IS REMARKABLE.. THERE IS ENOUGH THOUGH not a bit is wasted and it is much more tastefully cooked than we got in Ottawa.   The war bread isn't bad at all and we are quite used to oleo now.  We get porridge, cocoa and some kind of meat or fish bread and oleo for breakfast
column21: soup, meat, and potatoes and another vegetable, bread and pudding for dinner
column22: tea, meat and hash of some kind, bread , oleo and tarts or cakes and cookies for supper.  There is more flavor and attractiveness to the food than I ever dreamed of getting in the army.........""   FROM A LETTER WRITTEN BY L.-CPL. C.M.CROFT  ""I WAS VERY GLAD TO   receive my Christmas Argosy on Tuesday night.  It seems to bring back ""old Mt.A"" days and the excellent times there.  I, for one did not appreciate to any great extent just what good times we were having until I got away"" . . . . . . . . . . . .FROM A LETTER RECEIVED FROM PTES. E.C.DUFF,'19 AND J.C.JORDAN, '17 . . . . WE WERE VERY pleasantly surprised during the week to receive the box from old Mt.A. and were very glad to know that we are not forgotten by the few who are left .  . . . . During our stay in London we found ourselves among old friends - thanks to Professer Tweedie's English classes - such places as  St, Pauls, Westminster Abby, London Bridge, Drury Lane,  and the Horse Guards,  recalled some of the periods spent in his classes........LETTER FROM N.COLL '17 TO PROFESSOR LINE "" nO DOUBT YOU HAVE HEARD OF the Canadian Khaki College  established by former Canadian professors, now in khaki. It extends over England and France and we are told that all work is credited by Canadian Colleges. . . The library is fair and I plan to register to get the use of it and so will be able to review some Hebrew Grammar. Professor Watson might have to say I have to learn it before  I can review it.  That is more like it too.  . . . .  . . . . . . WE QUOTE THE FOLLOWING FROM A LETTER FROM COL. JOSEPH HAYES, '84-'85... . . . . AT DAYBREAK ONE MORNING Capt. Hensley took another young officer with him, Lieut. Hollett, and undertook a reconnaisance of the position.   They crept to the very top of this perilous position and peered over the German garrison, who were busy making breakfast almost within arms length of them.   At this moment they were discovered and a heavy fire quickly opened on them.  They scampered down the slope and back to their dugout headquarters unscathed and rollicking over their adventure as boys would over an animated snowball scrap on the college campus
column23: only their clothes were covered in mud and torn in a few places with bullets.   They had secured valuable information which was passed on to the higher command and acknowledged with special thanks.......""",,"World War I

column27: Full Text,"FT: TEN YEARS FROM NOW YOU WILL appreciate what Mount Allison has done for you more than you do today.   I am anxious that you should be well informed as to the history of the college, that you may intelligently identify yourselves with her interests.  I want you to feel that the Insitutions you will call your Alma Mater, your foster mother, almost your second mother, is worthy of your true regard and your most loyal advocacy.   Absorbing the high ideals that are the very atmosphere of both class rooms and campus, emulating the splendid herioism of the 500 Mount Allison men who are still facing death for the sake of the great cause, it is yours to scorn all temptations to ease and self indulgence and by faithful performance of the daily task fit yourselves for entering the splendid heritage left you by students and teachers of other years.",,Mount Allison

column3: Full Text,"FT: THE EARLY CLOSING MAY cause some difficulties in connection with the customary closing exercises that have been in process of preparation for some time , but considering the  opportunities afforded for the men engaged in war-work of some sort, and especially the need of men in agriculture, we are sure those inconveniencedwill adapt thier plans uncomplainingly to accord with the new arrangements.   The request of the students to do war work has been met
column4: it now remains for them to make good their expressed intentions..",,Closing Exercises

column3: Full Text,"FT:  p. 278.   THIS PAST YEAR HAS BEEN ONE OF VARIED EXPERIENCES in Italy. Up until the end of October last our hospital was very busy and I had a great many surgical cases and operations.   At the time of the retreat, we lost everything that went to make the hospital and we had just completed an operating theatre of which we were justly proud,  as it was one of the best in that district.     Some of our cars had to be abandoned and most of us lost our personal effects.   My wife was out in the same district nursing is a Smitamento (clearing station) and I had to rescue her on the back of a motor cycle.   The week that followed was a nightmare and it has even yet its horrors.   Twice my wife and I were in imminent danger of being captured and it was by a fortunate knowledge of the side roads that we got thru at all
column4: more than ever we felt that Providence does have a hand in our destinies!   My wife is at present running a Soldier's Club for Tommies near - - -  I am acting Medical Officer for the BRCS Units (British Red Cross Socity Units] in Italy and in that capacity get over a good bit of the Front.  We have a station doing front line work both in the mountain and plain.  I have a car fitted up for all emergencies, even to a ""Tabloid"" Operating Theatre. . . . CAPT.W.E. THOMPSON . . . . . . . . . .|p. 280  I HEARD ABOUT POOR GORDON ABOUT TWO WEEKS AGO BUT HAVE BEEN HARDLY ABLE to believe it.  I am afraid we will never play any more hockey over your head at 3 a.m.   GNR. K. AUSTIN TAYLOR",,"Thompson, W.E. Dr. Captain
column21: |W.P. Bell Collection of Acadiana, Microform Room
column24: Full Text,"FT:  p. 286:   MANY CHANGES HAVE TAKEN PLACE in the student body since the first 'merry gentlemen' marched away to the wars.   We do not refer alone to the difference in numbers, nor altogether to a partial change in the interests of the student
column25: they are undoubtedly broader in scope that formerly, and must necessarilty be so when the student's interest in his studies or in the winning of a game of football or hockey is mingled with a deeper concern for the welfare of comrades on the far battle line.   But to whatever cause it may be due, the boys do not sing as they were wont to do in the 'good old day' before the war clouds lowered.  Tje boys used to sing and the halls of the residence echoed to their melody, but the old order seems to have changed and the crowd no longer gathers in the lower hall , before commencing work in the evening, to join in the old college songs.   At other times, when it is felt the songs should be sung, the attempt is perfunctory and satisfying neither as concerted action nor as harmony.  . . . . . .",,"MacDonald, T.A. (Mae) Sergeant MM

column13: Full Text,"FT: p. 361   MANY OF OUR NUMBER who would have been with us today had not the call to service elsewhere found them ready, are in the trenches and some have paid the great sacrifice.   In their memory, we the remaining members of the class present to the University, this  Service Flag as a token that they are not forgotten. THE FINANCIAL LOSS, HOWEVER, CONSEQUENT UPON SUCH A DECREASED ATTENDANCE, OTHER INSTITUTIONS WITH LARGER . . . .  .endowments may have been able to carry with greater ease.   The increased cost of provisions and coal has resulted in a large increase in expenditure over that of last year.   This has been only partially met by the slight increase in charges for board made to resident students.   In order to meet this War deficit a special appeal has been made through the Alumni Society to friends of Mount Allison and the students of other years to raise a fund to tide over present difficulties.   This fund has been called a Loyalty Fund and bases it claim for support upon such broad patriotic reasons as drawing upon the sympathies of men everywhere today.   BYRON CRANE BORDEN, PRESIDENT . .  . . . . .",,"World War I

column1: The war is over and Mount Allison rejoices.     In the commemoration of Europe's greatest event Mount A was unique in that she  preceded the celebration by a fitting rehearsal.   Three days previous to the arrival of the phenepotentarie  [plenipotentiary?] in France, a report was circulated that the war was over.   No one could be expected to be hesitant or sane enough to question its authority, the result was, soon Old Mount A. was in the throes of unrestrained excitement. . . .  . On Oct. 11 th 5 a.m. we were really assured that the armistice had been assigned.   It was with thankful hearts that we arose from our beds at the shrill command of the sirens and the earnest peal of the town bells. . . . . . .At 2:30 p.m. a devotional service was held upon the college campus . . . . Then the students joined with the citizens of the town in a parade.  The two noteable features of the parade were, first, the several decorated floats of which, that representing Mt.Allison was by no means  least,  secondly, the banner containing the Honour Roll of the names of fifty-three Mount Allison men.   The many illuminated automobiles added considerably to the appearance of the evening procession.  . . . . . .  The day of reverlries was brought to a close by a display of fire works and a bon fire on the Ladies College campus, and the burning of the Kaiser in effigy - ""hit him again."" . . . . .|P. 85  LADIES COLLEGE. IN SPITE OF THE FACT THAT  on Oct. 11 the Ladies College was in strict quarantine, the reception of the season was held on that evening... . . . . . .|p. 86. The first social event to take place after the lifting of the quarantine was a reception, which was held in Beethoven Hall on Friday evening, November 8.   Perhaps the outstanding characteristic of the reception was the fact that the number of boys present far exceeded the number of girls.",,World War I

column18: Full Text,"FT:  THE MOUNT ALLISON MEMORIAL LIBRARY . . . IT IS ALTOGETHER FITTING AND PROPER THAT SOMETING SHOULD BE DONE TO COMMEMORATE the glorious part which Mount Allison has played in the Great War, now happily ended.   For love of country over five hundred Mount Allison men and women enlisted for service and of these, sixty six accepted death and thus made immortal their patriotism and their virtue.   To those of us,  who for a brief time, are spared to take part in the conflict of life, there comes the clrear call to do something which will worthily show our appreciation for the noble and heroic deeds of Allisonians, whi gave their lives that juctice and right might prevail, that the world might be made safe for democrarcy . . . . .",,Mount Allison Memorial Library

column3: Full Text,"FT:  p. 1.   We devote this issue of the Record to our men and women in the war because of the great interest of Mount Allison people in general in the contribution of their college to the cause which we support.   .    .    .    .   . We shall fail in our appreciation of their service if we do not honor in an adequate and dignified way, those sons who in the pursuit of duty have given their lives on the battlefields of Flanders.   There are many ways in which it might be done.  .   .    .    . One of our most pressing needs at Mount Allison today is a library.  .   .   .   .",,World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT p. 1.  .  .  .  . WHO WOULD THINK THAT OUT OF TWO HUNDRED AND FORTY ENLISTED MEN  this college would send into the army, in officers alone, five Colonels,  two of them Brigadiers,  Fourteen majors,   and sixty-five Lieutenants?  He who knows anything of the technical knowledge that an officer , especially of the higher ranks must possess, will not cease to marvel at these figures.   It is a justification of the existence of the College that its sons are the men who most readily master the arts of war . . . . . . So it is with the non-commissioned officers.  Most of our men who are not officers of this class, the men who in every army constitute its backbone. And as we look over the list, we see them as the youngsters of yesterday. And youngsters, often, they are compared with the men they lead.   Those qualities of leadership which place them in command of others, the College must have cultivated else all the signs are false.    And those who still are privates have remained so by choice, or need but the opportunity to show the same qualities as their friends.  .. . . . . .P. 10.....LIEUT. JOHN WIGGINS:   HE ENLISTED IN THE 9TH WINNIPEG RIFLES  early in the war.   In the early summer of 1915, while on furlough,  he visited the Cloth Hall at Ypres which at that time was largely destroyed and there in a dugout he found a copy of the Mount Allison Argosy. He never knew who left it there.  Shortly after he was gassed at Ypres and spent some time in a hospital and later was given a position in the Audit Office . On complete recovery, he took a course for a commission and was appointed to the 54th Batt., in which he was acting Adjutant. Recently, after two months in the trenches he was wounded again.",,"Military Service

column3: Full Text,"FT. p. 5. MY BROTHER SHENTON S. KING, AC. '00-01, comes properly under the heading ""Sick and Wounded""  He has had a quite eventful military career . He is a doctor as you probably know,  He volunteered at the very beginning, took his course in Halifa, 1914, was called overseas in March (Ithink) 1915, came over in April, was for some time at Salisbury Plain then went to Egypt (Base Hospital Alexandria), later to Gallipoli, was on boat service between there and Malta for some time, was at the evauation of Anzac and Suvla Bay, and then sent to Greece, then as Medical officer of some Lancaster Battalion (he had been transferred in England to R.A.M.C.), he took part in the Towsnhend Relief Expedition,   was invalided to Bombay, operated on, sent to England, thence to Canada, was operated on at R.V. Hospital was at home for a few months.",,"World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT Page 8 LETTER FROM JON E. BROOKS, EASTPORT MAINE, NOV. 24TH, 1917. TO THE EDITOR OF THE REDORD,SACKVILLE NB SIR---Enclosed find one dollar for which send me the Record.  I have had a few copies from time to timeand am indebted to Guy Humphrey for several copies and they are interesting.     I am sorry to hear of David Jardine's death, but he died as he had often wished that he might die, fighting for his God, his Country and is Ideals.   I will await further news of Capt. George R. Johnson and trust that he will make a sppedy recovery.   I am glad to hear that George R. McCord is home for a needed rest.   So on I might go through the list.   As each copy of the Record has come to me I have read the name of this one or that one doing his all and giving his all for preservation of the Nation's Life, and as the memory of the old days at Mt Allison arise in happy recollection, I see the promise of the boys of 20 years ago coming to fullfillment.   To have known such men as jardine, Johnson and McCord gives ona feeling of pride which Time cannot efface.",,"World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT:   MANY PEOPLE HAVE WONDERED WHAT EFFECT THE WAR COULD HAVE ON THE COLLEGE MEN who had left their work uncompleted to enlist in the fighting forces.   Some have thought that the out-of-door life, the freedom from close mental application to a sedentary task and the development of an adventurous spirit which perhaps goes with the life of the army, would make it difficult , if not impossible, for those men to settle down to a life of study.   It has been feared too, that would would not desite to again take up their college work. Time will only tell.   It may be interesting to note, however, that we have at Mount Allison four returned men, Barrett, Styles, Pike, and Butler, and that, apparently, these men have dropped into the routine of college life and work with the greatest ease.  Judging from the quality of their work, they have returned to their tasks with an added seriousness and a keener appreciation of the value of a college course.  Though their numbers are far too few to give evidence of much value their example may be a hopeful omen.",,Military Service

column3: Full Text,"FT P.2  WORD HAS JUST BEEN RECEIVED THAT MAJOR CARTER HAS BEEN AWARDED THE DISTINUISHED SERVICE ORDER.   He went overseas in the 26th, was wounded, sent home and went back as a company Commander in the 140th. He transferred to the Royal Flying Corps and some time ago he was made a Squadron Commander.   His latest honour has just been gazetted.",,World War I

column1: 5997,object05997,record,,,,,Sick and Wounded:  Lieut. J.D. MacLean the writer of the following .  . .,,Mount Allison Record,,,,Vol.II No.5,"1918, February",p.3-4,Periodicals
column3: Full Text,"FT:  p. 3     Ist Can Reserve Batt., Seaford Sussex, Eng.     OUR BATTALION THE 3RD CANADIAN PIONEERS (48TH CEF) WAS ONE OF THE BC UNITS THAT WAS BROKEN UP LAST SPRING.     Half of us were sent to reinforce the 7th and the other half the 29th.  I went to the 7th on the 17th of May last.   My stay wasn't a lenghty one by any means.   After about an hour and a half after the beginning of the Hill 70 show, one of our rifle grenades dropped short and I got it in the right arm and side.   It wasn't serious but it knocked the wind out of me
column4: so much so I am told that I looked green.   Consequently the report was spread that I was all-in.   Ever since I have had an interesting time proving that I wasn't.     I am glad that the University is still going strong.   The Christmas Argosy arrived a few days ago and I expect the Record will arrive shortly.   It is hard to realize that we have been over here for two years,   I was sorry to hear that Dr. Wheelock had gone to Acadia.  I thought that he sould finally be converted to staying with Mount Allison.   I hope that Professors McKiel and Crowell are still going strong.  I found Professor Crowell's letter in the stuff that was taken out of my pockets when I was hit.   Earl Spicer and Arthur Moss are both in London at the Record Office.   We had dinner together last Staturday. Quite a reunion it was.   Gerry Anglin has got a Bar to his Military Cross.   He and Ray Dill are at present at the Prince of Wales Hospital,  London.    Gerry's leg was broken in the same place as in the football match with Kings in 1014.   Dill was gassed.  Carl Pickard, Calkin and Metherall are at the New Brunswick Reserve here.  Paton has just left for the 26th.  Murdock and I had dinner with them one night shortlyafter Christmas.   ""Paddy"" O. Bartlett was killed
column5: at the Cambrai show, I think it was.   His bar to his Military Cross was Gazetted a short time ago.   He made a splendid officer.   Sharp and Withrow I saw today. They just arrived a short time ago.   One of Professor Hunton's sons is with them I think.  Jordan and some other chaps that are going to the Princess Pats are somewhere near here.   Frank Fraser's brother is scout officer in the 7th Battalion in France.    I was quite surprised to find that out.   I saw Harold Hanson one day lately at the AG's office (Adjutant -General) in London.   Dexter, who went to the Academy, I saw in the 58th last spring
column6: the same day I saw Herb Poole and Joe Sutherland.   The latter, I think have their commissions in the 58th.   I was sorry to hear of Hensley's death.   He was doing very well.   Ruggles left for Canada over a month ago.  Mersereau is here with the Kilties.   I just missed seeing Baines '11, last spring.   Hugh Eaton is here, I believe, although I haven't seen him yet.   Mrs. Gamblin, originally Jean Spicer, is in Seaforth at present.   I had hoped that ""Wopp"" Borden would be among the Signallers draft so that I could look him up to see the ""Queen"".   Charlie Thompson is here on a Pioneers  course, saw him this afternoon  .    Please give my good wishes to everybody.    Sincerely, Jas. D. Maclean",,"World War I

column1: 5998,object05998,record,,,,,The following letter is from Wilfred Dawson '14 . . . .,,Mount Allison Record,,,,Vol.II No.5,"1918, February",p.4,Periodicals
column3: Full Text,"FT:  p. 4     IN THE FIELD
column4: THE LAST FEW DAYS HAVE BEEN QUITE COLD AND CLEAR AND THE MUD HAS BECOME LIKE IRON.    The nights are getting pretty long and I often think of Julius Caesar's little phrase in the Commentaries ' "" In Hibernis"".  With the Russian front gone quiet, we may not have much going on in winter quarters here.     There doesn't seem much to write about.  I have no doubt that history is being made here  but it is unpleasant stuff to handle red hot.    Perhaps you know Albert Dennis. He has jst returned from a leave to England.   Ours ought to be coming along soon.   We're beginning to ""sweat on it"" as the Tommies say.  Myself I'd sure like a trip to Paris.  They say it is a ""bon place"".     It is curious how many French words we've picked up in our stay in the Field.    Curious pronunciations too.    ""tres bon""  is ""tray bon"", tribon. trebon. Trays beans, or thee beans vaguely resembles ""Tres biens"" . ""Beaucoup"" is distorted in to ""boko, boca, or bekoo"" .  Ca ne fait rien is not so popular. I was quite proud, I remember, one evening of learning from a village urchin the equivalent of ""That's the stuff to g'ie 'em"",  ""C'est ca pour leu donner"".    I like the French.  They are very kind and seem to like talking to us and to appreciate our being here.    While I am on the subject of language, I must say that the slang of the Canadians is an endless source of amusement and wonder to the English men.   ""You fellows do have some queer sayings"".  The English have some slang too, but they confine their efforts when excited, usually  to the the production of cursing rather than complicated slang.   They'll be absolutely lost when the Americans begin to circulate.   Not that some English men haven't slang, too. At our O.P. was with a party of men from an English battery formed in 1914.  They's say fror example, ""when I rumbled how that nib was bumming the chat""  is being interpretd, ""when I understood how that particular specimen of the genus homo was monopolizing the converstion""   W.T. Dawson",,"World War I

column3: Full Text,"FT:  p. 6 OF ALL THE CHAPLINS IN FRANCE NONE HAS PLAYED THE PART OF THE SOLDIER MORE than has Capt. Clarke.   He came to France nearly two years ago and has been in the front lines in every battle the Canadians have been engaged in during that time.   He trained originally as a combattant officer and was for five months in an Infantry Battalion  in command of an infantry platoon.     He knew his drill thoroughly and was considered a first rate instructor.    He was, however, appointed as a chaplain of the 2nd Pioneers two years ago.   After the Battle of Vimy Ridge, Capt. Clarke went over the front area of the entire Division, looking for and burying the bodies of the dead heroes who fell in the fight.    This was a critical and dangerous task as the Germans kept up an  almost continuous shell fire for days after the battle.   Capt Clarke has been twice on the Belgian Front.  In 1916 he was through the Battle of St. Eloi and in 1917 through the battle of Passchendale.   He was also through the Somme and the Battle of Vimy Ridge.  He had charge of the coffee stalls of the forward area on the Somme.   He lived in a dugout on army rations at Pozliers and served seven thousand soldiers daily with free coffee.   He was for seven consecutive weeks under more or less constant shell fire at that time and had several narrow escapes.. . . .",,"World War I

column3: Full Text,FT P.6.    . . . .WHAT DO YOU THINK OF THE PROPOSED VIMY RIDGE UNIVERSITY?    I was talking to a YMCA Officer about it and he told me that the main work would commence during the armistice and from that time on eight months' courses in all subjects would be given and the year recognized by all Canadian universities.   No doubt you know more of the the matter than I do.   I introduced Dr. Cohoon to him and hope that the former will find employment in this work.,,"World War I

6041,object06041,record,,,,,Mount Allison Memorial Library,,Mount Allison Record,,,,Vol III no 5,February 1919,p.33-39,Microform;Periodicals,FT Excerpt from speech of Desbarres the university Librarian, page 37 . . . . Like all really good libraries, ours brings us into association with the great minds of the world. But beyond that it brings us into touch with the Mount Allison Tradition.     No one could see our young men go out from us to the Great War, one after the other, until you count them by the hundreds, no one could read the record of their deeds upon the field of battle,  and in all too many cases learn the story of their deaths,  without being sure of that.  There is a strong Mount Allison tradition and strong in it is the sense of responsibility and the principle of service. . . .",,"World War I

6062,object06062,record,,,,,The Seniors At Home,,Mount Allison Record,,,,Vol III no 7,April 1919,p.50,Microform;Periodicals,FT: p. 50-51     After four years the Senior's At Home has agin found its place in the Social Life of the University.   Discontinued becuse of its expense and inappropriateness during war times, it recalled to many the names and faces of those who in earlier days had gaily played their part in similar events and who today are counted among the men Mount Allison will ever honour, for the great sacrifice they have made for justice and right.    Cheerfully, and with great expectations they accepted their obligations for the ""Great Show"" just as they did those for their own ""Seniors"" and to them both were great adventures.",,At Homes

6080,object06080,record,,,,,Dr. Borden's Report,,Mount Allison Record,,,,Vol III nos 8-9,May-June 1919,p.62-65,Microform;Periodicals,FT. p. 62    "" We were fortunate in being able to get through  with the influenza epidemic, Which swept over the Colleges early in the year and broke up the work of many, without having to suspend our classes. It is a matter of great gratification and should be appreciated by the patrons and friends of our educational institutions that, not withstanding the fact that over 100 cases of this fatal disease developed in our Institutions, under the blessings of God, we were able to get through without the loss of a single life.   Apart from this epidemic, the general health of the students has been excellent and notwithstanding the temporary inconvenience suffered through the development of a light case of smallpox in the University Residence later in the achool year,our work has not been seriously interfered with.     We have had a total registration of 166.   Of these it has been our pleasure to welcome back to MOunt Allison some twenty or more of our undergraduates who had volunteered in the service of the Empire.   While there has been much talk about the nervous unrest of our returned men and the difficulty they have experienced in settling down to the dull routine of daily life, it is a great testimony to the fine character and studious habits of our returned boys . I cannlot recall a single instance where the men from overseas have not returned to us better men than when they left us.          P. 63:    The great sacrifice has been made, The memory f it, however, is with us to inspire to deeds of unselfishness and heroic service.   The need of such a service is evident during these days of reconstruction when our world is recovering from the delirium of our four years' whirlwind of war.   The least we can do is to cherish the memory and honor the names of our men who have fallen.  A movement to erect a fitting memorial to the men who have served and the men who have fallen during the war has been inaugurated by the Alumni and Alumnae Societies under the firection of the BOard of Regents.   .  .   .   .   .  .  . p. p.64:   While the year has been one of marked success and the educational results have been gratifying
column8: while the income from the student's accounts has been about $7000.00 in advance of last year
column9: while the outlook for the future was never brighter than it is today
column10: while new fields of educational effort are opening up in every direction
column11: while the call of the hour is for an education that is practical as well as cultural
column12: while the College, like the aeroplane flotilla assembled at Newfoundland, seems to be waiting for favourable conditions to wing its was to new conquests, it seems a sordid and unworthy anti-climax to  say that the friends of Mount Allison will have to face their educational problems in a bigger way and put themselves loyaly and unselfishly at the back of our educational work, if Mount Allison is to hold her place in the front rank of Maritime Universities.   The fact, however, remains and will have to be faced.",,Epidemics

column4: Full Text,"FT:  Excerpt p. 10   I have always had reason to be proud of being a graduate of Mount Allison University.  Here, a lIberal education has been and still is offered, an education not necessaritly connected with making a living but with developing ones faculties.   Here specialization does not begin too soon, only after some training in English, History, Philosophy, Mathematics and a foreign language.   In my day, and I am sure it is the same  today, Mount Allison was in truth a University, that is, and association of students and professors meeting with the object of acquiring knowledge already existing and expanding its boundaries. . . . . .",,"West, Frank L.

column4: Full Text,"FT: p. 34-35 excerpt.    Mount Allison?  Who can say what she is? We're up against the old difficulty with words  . . . . I think of all the little inconcequential things I'Ve talked about  . . . .of successes and failures . . . hopes and dreams . . .realized and unrealized.   And I think of a middle-aged farmer - fisherman, following along a sidehill in the cold spring days, . . .or salting down mackerel on the beach . . . and thinking of his boy away at school.    That too is Mount Allison.",,"Bruce, Charles

column3: Photographs,"FT: In his address at Founder's Day,1954,  Roy Fraser said:

column1: To each of us will come back again and again, things which we would be shy about putting into words, perhaps because no one else can understand how we feel about them. We don't undertand it ourselves.

The sunlight on the water over by Beausejour, - the big white clouds sailing up the Bay of Fundy, like the billowing white sails of the old clipper ships that were the glory of these provinces, - the kindly folk of this good town, and their gracious hospitality to young people far from home, - the little yellow August flowers, dancing in the Tantramar wind, - the exquisite woodsy frangrance of a little bunch of Mayflowers on a spring day,-the spot on the campus where you said goodby to a boy who went to war and didn't come back, - the coloured lights of the campus Chistmas tree glowing through the falling snow, - the voices of the Senoir class singing in their last chapel, - -the voice of the President conferrring a degree, -, the ache in your throat and the blur in your eyes as you turn to look back at the old hill for the last time.

Sentiment"" you say?  Certainly! Subtract from life the things that men and women love today, and hold in tender recollection tomorrow - and what possible meaning or worth could there be in what is left?",,"Flemington, Ross

To each of us will come back again and again, things which we would be shy about putting into words, perhaps because no one else can understand how we feel about them. We don't undertand it ourselves.

The sunlight on the water over by Beausejour, - the big white clouds sailing up the Bay of Fundy, like the billowing white sails of the old clipper ships that were the glory of these provinces, - the kindly folk of this good town, and their gracious hospitality to young people far from home, - the little yellow August flowers, dancing in the Tantramar wind, - the exquisite woodsy fragrance of a little bunch of Mayflowers on a spring day,-the spot on the campus where you said goodby to a boy who went to war and didn't come back, - the coloured lights of the campus Chistmas tree glowing through the falling snow, - the voices of the Senior class singing in their last chapel, - -the voice of the President conferrring a degree, - the ache in your throut and the blur in your eyes as you turn to look back at the old hill for the last time.

Sentiment"" you say?  Certainly! Subtract from life the things that men and women love today, and hold in tender recollection tomorrow - and what possible meaning or worth could there be in what is left?",,"Fraser, Roy

column5: But then, as now, a centre of attraction was the ladies College. The standard of female pulchritude in those days was very high, so it seemed to us and many a boy discovered he had a ""cousin"", often of very remote relationship with whom he succeeded in having a ""private reception"", and many a cupid's dart found permamnent lodgement and caused many a wedding march in days to come.    . . . The Preceptress was Mrs. Mary Archibald, a lady whom all Mt.Allison students held in highest regard, both for her scholarship attainments and for her wise and diplomatic handling of many a difficult situation.   She is remembered too, as the mother of that distinuished son of the campus and generous friend of the university and one of America's leading mathmaticians. He was then a small lad in velvet suit who early developed a remarkable talent as a violinist.. . . . . . . .The annual Dorchester Drive was an institution.  Each man who cared to (and could spare the necessary collatoral) invited a White House young lady for a sleigh drive to the neighbouring town  where we were shown through the penitentiary - perhaps to remind us that our lots might be worse than wresting with trigonomery and Greek verbs. Dances and Proms were not yet dreamed of but in their place was the reception when we promenaded up and down the narrow hall of the Ladies College or sat it out on the stairs, often asking such inane questions as:""Is this your first term?"" ot ""How do you like your teachers?"" In a short timre we graduated to the discussion of more interesting topics.""",,"Hewson, E.E.
column36: |W.P. Bell Collection of Acadiana, Microform Room

column4: Full Text,"FT: P. 16 . . . Did we break any rules in those days? Well, , of course, a midnight supper in the rooms of a friend who had just received a box was a temptation, and sometimes successful. Then too, sometimes after dinner, before afternoon classes began, some very daring girls prhaps six or eight, would go to Beethoven and dance by themselves but this proved disastrous so  many times that it was given up, for it meant that the culprits must confine thier walks to the grounds in the afternoons and were not allowed to go to any concert or reception. . . .  . . I wonder if you of today enjoy your dances any more than we did our receptions.  I doubt it.  Our topics were asked for just as are your dances:  we did not have to discuss any particular topic as you might imagine.  A bell sounded at the beginning of each topic, instead of your orchestera.  Our partners found us and we walked from the lower end of Beethoven, through the narrow hall past the well and down the main hall as far as Dr. Borden's Parlour, then back to Beethoven and the same thing over and over again.  . . . . .",,"Ladies College

column1: 6657,object06657,record,,,,,Alumni Day,"Tweedie, William Morely",Mount Allison Record,,,,Vol. XX no. 3,"1937, March-May",pp.82-98.,Periodicals,"FT: p. 84     As I look back I recall many figures once prominent at Mount Allison who seem so much part of it that one could hardly fancy Mount Allison without them.   They vanished and left a blank or an ache for certain individuals, but the college community went on unperturbed.   It is fortunate that time closes these gaps.   We have to remember that the college generation is short. lived.    A student who returns after a year or two finds few familiar faces, and after four or five years the student body is completely changed.   Through this continual flux it is easy to drop into forgetfulness.   For example if you were to go through the present student body, I fancy you would find few who knew anythng or perhaps ever heard of Dr. Smith, Dr. Inch, Dr. Allison Names familiar to many of us aand brnging up cherished memories.   Dozens of other who helped to build up the Mount Allison of today have  'fallen into the darkened crypt of vanished forms and faces'.  That was true of our founder himself, Charles F. Allison, until a number of years ago somebody suggested that each year there should be a celebration of Founder's Day to keep successive generations of students reminded of the man whose vision, energy and fortune made the beginnings of Mount Allison.""",,"Tweedie, William Morely

We are also anxious that you should sense the fact that you are not here only to complete your studes and geta degree, but to be assisted towards that higher ideal of womanhood, for which Mount Allion stands.   It is essentially a place for character building, and we are eager that you should go out from these classic halls not only with scholasitic qualifications, but with the character that will mark you culturally, and with a taste for all that is finest in life.""",,Alumnae

column4: There was no Christmas vacation. Of course, Christmas day and new Year's day were holidays, but we were not encouraged to break up our work to visit our homes At Christmas. They were far away and the trip was expensive and slow.   There was a great deal of stage-coach, and it was thought better that we should stay at the institution.   There was always a New Year's Day drive to Amherst or Dorchester, when those who coud afford it would drive Their single horse team and each have the company of his chosen lady.   Those who could not afford this luxury would find accomodation in one or other of the large teams driven by Dr. Pickarf or one of the professors.""",,"Russell, Benjamin
